Subtractive Mixture Models via Squaring:
Representation and Learning

Lorenzo Loconte, Aleksanteri M. Sladek, Stefan Mengel, Martin Trapp, Arno Solin, Nicolas Gillis, Antonio Vergari

University of Edinburgh, UK
Aalto University, FI
Université d'Artois, CNRS, CRIL, FR
Université de Mons, BE
github.com/subtractive-mixtures

0 Mixture models

p(X) = Σ w_i p_i(X) subject to w_i ≥ 0

Fewer components with subtractions

Questions? ...Contributions!

  • 1 How to learn subtractive mixture models?
  • 2 How much more expressive are they?
  • 3 What is their relationship with other models?

1 How to learn subtractive mixture models?

p(X) = Σ w_i p_i(X) where w_i ∈ ℝ

How to ensure p(X) is non-negative? Impose ad-hoc constraints or use squaring.

TL;DR

"We learn exponentially more expressive mixture models with subtractions, by squaring deep tensorized mixtures"

1 Squaring mixtures ...

Tractable marginalization is supported by exponential families and splines components.

2 Build deep mixtures with layers as "Lego blocks"

Theorem. exponential separation

There is a class of distributions F over variables X that can be compactly represented as a shallow squared mixture with negative weights, but the smallest structured decomposable additive-only mixture of any depth computing any F has size 2^Ω(|X|).

3 Understanding the expressiveness of other models

Density estimation results across various datasets (Power, Gas, Hepmass, MiniBooNE, BSDS300).

GPT2 distillation

References

[1] B. Zhang and C. Zhang. "Finite mixture models with negative components". In: MLDM. Springer, 2005.
[2] G. Rabusseau and F. Denis. "Learning negative mixture models by tensor decompositions". In: arXiv preprint, 2014.
[3] R. Jiang, M. J. Zuo, and H. Li. "Weibull and inverse Weibull mixture models allowing negative weights". In: Reliability Engineering, 2019.
[4] J. Martens and V. Medabalimi. "On the expressive efficiency of sum product networks". In: arXiv preprint, 2014.
[5] A. de Colnet and S. Mengel. "A Compilation of Succinctness Results for Arithmetic Circuits". In: KR, 2021.
[6] I. Glasser et al. "Expressive power of tensor-network factorizations for probabilistic modeling". In: NeurIPS, 2019.
[7] A. Rudi and C. Ciliberto. "PSD Representations for Effective Probability Models". In: NeurIPS, 2021.
[8] H. Zhang et al. "Tractable Control for Autoregressive Language Generation". In: ICML, 2023.

Deconvolving Detector Effects for Distribution Moments

Krish Desai, Benjamin Nachman, Jesse Thaler

UC Berkeley, LBNL, MIT
krish.desai@berkeley.edu

Abstract

  • Deconvolving detector distortions – critical step to compare cross section measurements with theoretical predictions
  • Most approaches require binning
  • Theoretical predictions often at the level of moments.
  • New approach to directly unfold distribution moments as a function of other observables without discretizing data
  • Moment Unfolding uses a modified GAN architecture
  • Demonstrate the performance of this approach using jet substructure measurements in collider physics.

Background

  • Unfolding (deconvolution): correcting detector distortions in experimental data – necessary for accurately comparing data between experiments and with theoretical predictions.
  • Typically, entire spectra unfolded – moments computed afterward.
  • Current approaches discretize support—then unfold histogram.
  • This binning procedure introduces discretization artifacts.
  • Unfold without binning – generic solution to unfolding entire spectra – may compromise precision for small set of moments.
  • Dedicated machine learning-based unfolding method to directly unfold the observable moments.
  • GAN learns reweighting function inspired by the Boltzman equation – parameters identified with observable moments.
  • Noniterative in contrast to methods like Omnifold (Andreassen et al, Phys. Rev. Lett. 124, 182001 – 2020)

Neural Networks

Generator: g(x) = eλ₁x + ... + λₙxⁿ reweights generation to truth. n trainable parameters λ, learn n moments

Discriminator: d : ℝ → [0, 1] distinguishes reweighted simulation from data

  • Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution maximizes entropy while holding mean energy constant
  • Moment unfolding maximises BCE loss holding moments constant

Data sets

  • Simulation (XS) : detector level simulation
  • Generation (XG) : particle level simulation
  • Data (XD) : detector level data
  • Truth (XT) : particle level data

Machine Learning Implementation

Loss Function

L[g, d] = -1/N Σ [log(d(xD)) + Σ g(xG) log(1 - d(xS))]
  • All neural networks implemented using the Keras high-level API with Tensorflow2 backend, optimized with Adam.
  • Discriminator d parametrized with three hidden layers, 50 nodes per layer. Intermediate layers → ReLU, last layer → sigmoid.

Gaussian Case Study

Gaussian distributions

  • XT ~ N(0.5, 1)
  • XG ~ N(0, 1)
  • detector effects Z ~ N(0, 5)

Jet Substructure

Jet width

w = (1 / pT,jet) Σ pT,i ΔRi
  • pT,i = transverse momentum
  • ΔRi = angular distance from jet axis
  • Example focusses on jet width. Method can be applied to other jet observables like charge, mass, etc.
  • First two moments of the reweighted generation match truth well
  • Full distributions not statistically identical
  • This is because higher moments are relevant and are not the same between truth and generation.

Conclusions and Outlook

  • Proposed Moment Unfolding as a novel, flexible, unbinned, and non-iterative reweighting-based deconvolution method
  • Showed promising results when applied to both Gaussian datasets as well as detector data from the LHC
  • Future work – whether this method could be used to unfold infinitely many moments, i.e. entire probability density
  • Important questions about partition function normalization, stability, and overlapping support

Data Analytics and Machine Learning Group
Department of Computer Science
Technical University of Munich

Revisiting Robustness in Graph Machine Learning

Lukas Gosch, Daniel Sturm, Simon Geisler, Stephan Günnemann

Zuse Schools
Konrad Zuse Schools of Excellence in AI

Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) suffer from over-robustness

  • Common threat models allow perturbations which change the node-level semantic content in graphs
  • GNNs are wrongly robust to these changes
  • Introduce semantic-aware notions of robustness
  • No robustness-accuracy tradeoff for inductively classifying a new node

On Adversarial Examples

  • Small perturbations (l_p-norm)
  • Change prediction of classifier f
  • Unchanged semantic content (e.g., image category)

"panda"

+ ε =

"gibbon"

Figure: Goodfellow et al. (2015)

What constitutes a small, semantics-preserving perturbation to a graph?

Commonly: Restrict number of inserted / deleted edges (l_0-norm)

Majority of nodes are low-degree → Large effect on neighborhood

Do common threat models preserve semantic content?

Use Random Graph Models

  • Exactly measure (node-level) semantic change

New Phenomenon

Over-robustness: Robustness beyond semantic change

Semantic-Aware Robustness

Idea: Use reference node classifier g to indicate semantic change.

Adversarial Example

A perturbed graph is adversarial if prediction changes but semantics stay same.

Over-robust Example

A perturbed graph is over-robust if prediction stays same but semantics change.

Theorem (informal)

There is no semantic-aware robustness-accuracy tradeoff for non-i.i.d. graph data when classifying a newly added node to a graph.

Experiments

Setup: Contextual stochastic block models (CSBM)

Table: Existence probability of graphs with changed semantics.

→ High prevalence of graphs with changed semantics!

How much of the measured robustness is in fact over-robustness?

→ Significant amount of conventional robustness is over-robustness

Take-Aways

  • Use strong attacks → Reduce the amount of over-robustness measured
  • Be cautious in applying l_0-norm restricted adversaries
  • More research into semantic-preserving perturbations necessary
https://www.cs.cit.tum.de/daml/revisiting-robustness/
May 1st - May 5th, 2023 - Kigali, Rwanda

BC: fit θ to a collection of demonstrations from expert policy π*, minimizing 1-step loss

ℓBC(θ) = Eexpert's (s,a) [ ||πθ(st) - π*(st)||2 ]

Usual view: BC reduces offline imitation learning to supervised learning

ℓBC(θ) ≤ ε
train on 1-step loss
→
JH(πθ) ≥ JH(π*) - C·ε
evaluate multi-step rollout rewards
Q1: When BC works, how big is C in practice?
Q2: When BC fails, must we improve the data? Or can we improve upon standard training?

Special case: pretraining vs. generation in LLMs

  • st : sequence of t tokens
  • at : append a single token
  • πθ : autoregressive language model

Gradient Variance Amplification

A1: Long-horizon rollouts can be extremely sensitive to small SGD fluctuations.

GVA: an empirical pathology in BC with NNs

small changes in θ due to minibatch noise

...causing small changes in 1-step ℓBC(θ)...

...and chaotic long-rollout rewards JH(πθ)

Evidence: (across locomotion tasks & archs)

fractal reward landscape

oscillations during training

(in the paper: accompanying quantitative studies)

Theory: vignettes in toy models

  • - marginally stable linear dynamics, linear π, π*
  • - "CliffLQR": halfspace reward → GVA
  • - SDE limit: EMA & LR schedules both work

Algorithmic mitigations for GVA

A2: Variance reduction techniques mitigate the oscillations; EMA is highly effective.

Exponential Moving Average

θt+1 ← θt - ηt ∇θℓBC(θ) (or Adam, etc.)
θ̄t+1 ← (1 - βt)θ̄t + βt · θt ,    use θ̄t for inference

Bonus: GVA & EMA in language generation

EMA improves & stabilizes LM iterates before learning rate cooldown

github.com/jigangk/ibc

Demonstration-free Autonomous Reinforcement Learning via Implicit and Bidirectional Curriculum

Jigang Kim1*, Daesol Cho1*, H. Jin Kim1

*Equal contribution, 1Seoul National University

Seoul National University

Motivation

  • Most RL methods assume an episodic setting where resets to the initial state are readily available at the end of each episode.
  • Autonomous learning of embodied agents involves continual interactions and existing methods often require costly external interventions such as human supervision, and scripted policies to simulate resets.
  • Recently, Autonomous RL (ARL) framework formalized the non-episodic RL setting but previous works on ARL rely on demonstration data to guide the learning process.
  • However, a truly autonomous agent should be able to learn from scratch without external interventions and prior data.

Contribution

  • We propose a demonstration-free ARL algorithm via Implicit and Bidirectional Curriculum (IBC) that consists of a conditionally activated auxiliary agent and a bidirectional goal curriculum.
  • To the best of our knowledge, IBC is the first non-episodic RL that can consistently learn without manual resets and demonstrations by leveraging curriculum learning.
  • IBC achieves state-of-the-art performance against previous methods, including even the ones that leverage prior data.

References

[1] Sharma, Archit, et al. "Autonomous Reinforcement Learning: Formalism and Benchmarking." ICLR 2021.
[2] Kakade, Sham, and John Langford. "Approximately optimal approximate reinforcement learning." ICML 2002.
[3] Sharma, Archit, et al. "Autonomous reinforcement learning via subgoal curricula." NeurIPS 2021.
[4] Sharma, Archit, et al. "A State-Distribution Matching Approach to Non-Episodic Reinforcement Learning." ICML 2022.

Overview

Implicit curriculum

Bidirectional curriculum

Forward goal
Backward goal

Methods

Implicit curriculum via an auxiliary agent

ρ0(s) : initial state dist.

ρ*(s) : target state dist.

sg* : target goals

Bidirectional goal curriculum optimization

max Vπ(T)
max [Vπ(T) - L · D(T, T*)]

Curriculum objective

min Σ w((s0, sg)i, τi)

Environments

Experiments

IBC vs. Baselines

Ablation study

Curriculum progress

Do You Remember? Overcoming Catastrophic Forgetting for Fake Audio Detection

Xiaohui Zhang, Jiangyan Yi, Jianhua Tao, Chenglong Wang, Chuyuan Zhang

Institute of Automation, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing Jiaotong University, Tsinghua University

Introduction

Current fake audio detection algorithms have achieved promising performances on most datasets. However, their performance may be significantly degraded when dealing with audio of a different dataset. The orthogonal weight modification to overcome catastrophic forgetting does not consider the similarity of genuine audio across different datasets.

Figure 1. The feature distribution of bona-fide and spoof audio.[1]

Contribution

  • We propose a regularized adaptive weight modification (RAWM) algorithm to overcome catastrophic forgetting. Our method can modify weight's direction according to the number of genuine and fake audio.
  • Although our method is inspired by the feature distribution similarity in fake audio detection, it can also be used in other related tasks.
[1] Ma, Haoxin, et al. "Continual learning for fake audio detection." arXiv preprint arXiv:2104.07286 (2021).

Proposed Method

On most fake audio detection datasets, under the same acoustic conditions, feature distributions of genuine audio are relatively more concentrated than the fake.

Figure 2. Schematic of SGD, OWM, and RAWM

Adaptive Weight Modification

We introduce a square matrix Q as a projector that is orthogonal to the P proposed by the OWM algorithm. The modified direction R of weights, introduced in our paper, can be written as:

R = Pnorm + mQnorm

where m is a constant; Pnorm and Qnorm are identity matrices normalized by P and Q, respectively.

Regularization

There are also a few datasets where genuine audio is collected from quite different acoustic conditions compared with others. As for these utterances, we use the teacher-student framework as a regularization term.

Regularized Adaptive Weight Modification

Considering a continual learning situation, the BP process of regularized adaptive weight modification can be written as:

Wi(i, j) = Wi(i-1, j) + γ(i, j)ΔWiBP(i, j)     j = 1

Wi(i, j) = Wi(i-1, j) + (1-η)Ri(j-1)γ(i, j)ΔWiBP(i, j) + ηΔWi,regBP(i, j)     j > 1

Experimental Results

Table 1. The EER(%) of our method compared with various methods for fake audio detection.

Table 2. The Acc(%) of our method compared with various methods for image recognition.

We compared our method with several wide-used continual learning methods for fake audio detection. Among the results of our experiments, our method achieves the best learning and overcoming forgetting.

Conclusion

  • As illustrated in experimental results, our method shows promising performance on fake audio detection, emotion recognition, and image recognition, even training on a few samples.
  • We have yet to study how to make the model learn the weight direction gradually in the process of training on new datasets without any constraint, and exploring generalization to related tasks will form the focus of our future studies.

MOCCASIN: Efficient Tensor Rematerialization for Neural Networks

Burak Bartan, Haoming Li, Harris Teague, Christopher Lott, Bistra Dilkina

Qualcomm AI Research, University of Southern California
bbartan@qti.qualcomm.com
github.com/qualcomm/moccasin

1. Introduction

  • The deployment and training of neural networks on edge computing devices pose many challenges.
  • The low memory nature of edge devices is often one of the biggest limiting factors encountered in the deployment of large neural network models.
  • Tensor rematerialization or recompute is a way to address high memory requirements for neural network training and inference.
  • In this paper we consider the problem of execution time minimization of compute graphs subject to a memory budget.
  • In particular, we develop a new constraint programming formulation called Moccasin with only O(n) integer variables, where n is the number of nodes in the compute graph.

4. Solution

Our solution is an optimization problem formulation where:

  • The main building block is the concept of output retention intervals which simplifies the problem formulation greatly.
  • Output retention intervals are used to model how long the output of a node is retained in memory.
  • We define C retention intervals for each node where each interval represents a rematerialization (i.e. recompute) of that node.
  • The starting and end times of the intervals are modeled as the decision variables of the optimization problem.
  • We then define the precedence and memory constraints using these decision variables.

2. Motivation and problem statement

Consider the example 10-node computation graph above, and assume:

  • Output sizes are 50 for nodes on the left, and 1 for the ones on the right.
  • Duration is 1 for all nodes.

Rematerialization: Discard output of node 1 after node 2 is computed and recompute it later.

5. Formal specification

The table shows performance comparison across different graph families (RL, RW, CM) under different memory budgets, comparing Checkmate MILP, Checkmate LP+Rounding, and our MOCCASIN method.

3. Prior work

The work of [1] develops a mixed integer linear programming (MILP) formulation to solve this problem.

  • Introduces Boolean matrices R, S, F for recompute, storage, memory deallocation.
  • Poor scalability to large computation graphs.

Key assumption: Don't rematerialize any node more than C times.

[1] Checkmate: Breaking the Memory Wall with Optimal Tensor Rematerialization, Proceedings of Machine Learning and Systems 2 (MLSys 2020). Paras Jain, Ajay Jain, Aniruddha Nrusimha, Amir Gholami, Pieter Abbeel, Joseph Gonzalez, Kurt Keutzer, Ion Stoica

6. Numerical results

Figure: Percentage of total duration increase against solve time in seconds for our proposed method (Moccasin) and Checkmate [1] on a real-world graph with n=442 nodes and m=1247 edges.

QUALCOMM AI RESEARCH IS AN INITIATIVE OF QUALCOMM TECHNOLOGIES, INC.

Perone"], "organizations": ["Woven Planet United Kingdom Limited, Woven Planet North America, Inc."], "github": "github.com/woven-planet/cw-erm", "figures": [{"fig_id": "fig_1", "description": "Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS) Logo", "figure_width": 504, "figure_height": 223}]}, "sections": [{"id": "section_1", "title": "Main Ideas and Motivation", "content": ["Behavior Cloning (BC) algorithms for training self-driving vehicles via pure Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) biases policy towards matching open-loop behavior.", "We debias the policy network by identifying important samples via a closed-loop evaluator.", "Training in three phases:Open-loop identification phaseError set construction and evaluation phaseUpsampling training phase", "Open-loop identification phase", "Error set construction and evaluation phase", "Upsampling training phase"], "tables": [], "figures": []}, {"id": "section_2", "title": "Our Contributions", "content": ["Motivate and propose a technique that leverages closed-loop evaluation metrics acquired from policy rollouts in a simulator to debias the policy network and reduce distributional differences.", "Evaluate CW-ERM on a challenging urban driving dataset in a closed-loop fashion to show significant improvements without requiring complex training methods.", "Show an important connection of our method to a family of methods that addresses covariate shift through density ratio estimation."], "tables": [], "figures": []}, {"id": "section_3", "title": "Methodology", "content": ["Stage 1 (Identification Policy):Train policy network via BC in open-loop using ERM.", "Stage 2 (Closed-Loop Simulation):Perform rollouts in closed-loop simulator. Collect metrics and identify the error set E.", "Stage 3 (Final Policy):Train new policy where scenes belonging to the error set are upweighted by a factor w."], "tables": [], "figures": []}, {"id": "section_4", "title": "References", "content": ["1. Liu et al. (2021). Just Train Twice: Improving Group Robustness without Training Group Information. ICML.2. Vitelli et al. (2022). Safetynet: Safe planning for real-world self-driving vehicles using machine-learned policies. ICRA.3. Scheel et al. (2022). Urban driver: Learning to drive from real-world demonstrations using policy gradients. CoRL."], "tables": [], "figures": []}]}
output:
CW-ERM: Improving Autonomous Driving Planning with Closed-loop Weighted Empirical Risk Minimization

Eesha Kumar, Yiming Zhang, Stefano Pini, Simon Stent, Ana Sofia Rufino Ferreira, Sergey Zagoruyko, Christian S. Perone

Woven Planet United Kingdom Limited, Woven Planet North America, Inc.
github.com/woven-planet/cw-erm

Main Ideas and Motivation

  • Behavior Cloning (BC) algorithms for training self-driving vehicles via pure Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) biases policy towards matching open-loop behavior.
  • We debias the policy network by identifying important samples via a closed-loop evaluator.
  • Training in three phases:
    • Open-loop identification phase
    • Error set construction and evaluation phase
    • Upsampling training phase

Our Contributions

  • Motivate and propose a technique that leverages closed-loop evaluation metrics acquired from policy rollouts in a simulator to debias the policy network and reduce distributional differences.
  • Evaluate CW-ERM on a challenging urban driving dataset in a closed-loop fashion to show significant improvements without requiring complex training methods.
  • Show an important connection of our method to a family of methods that addresses covariate shift through density ratio estimation.

Methodology

Stage 1 (Identification Policy): Train policy network via BC in open-loop using ERM.

Stage 2 (Closed-Loop Simulation): Perform rollouts in closed-loop simulator. Collect metrics and identify the error set E.

Stage 3 (Final Policy): Train new policy where scenes belonging to the error set are upweighted by a factor w.

Collision Avoidance Examples

Front collisions

Side collisions

References

1. Liu et al. (2021). Just Train Twice: Improving Group Robustness without Training Group Information. ICML.
2. Vitelli et al. (2022). Safetynet: Safe planning for real-world self-driving vehicles using machine-learned policies. ICRA.
3. Scheel et al. (2022). Urban driver: Learning to drive from real-world demonstrations using policy gradients. CoRL.

Department of Computer Science, Purdue University
github.com/ashiqur-rahman/Fourier-Equivariance

Truly Scale-Equivariant Deep Nets with Fourier Layers

Md Ashiqur Rahman, Raymond A. Yeh

Motivation

Scale Equivariance:
  • Scale-equivariance is crucial for consistent performance
  • Prior works did not consider aliasing, resulting in equivariance error
Can we design a perfect scale equivariant layer?
  • Maintain scale consistency while achieving good performance
Contributions:
  • Propose scale-equivariant Fourier Layer
  • End-to-end equivariance with non-linearity and pooling
  • Connect scale equivariance to classification task for improved scale consistency.

Introduction

Scaling Operation: Down-scaling a signal x can be performed by subsampling by a scaling factor.

Ideal Down-sampling: To avoid aliasing from subsampling, a low-pass filter h must be performed.

Our Approach

Scale Equivariant Deep Nets

Let g denote a deep net such that y = g(x). If this deep net g can be equivalently represented as a set of functions...

Spatially Localized Spectral Conv:
  • Spectral convolution is scale equivariant
  • Global in nature - ill fit for vision tasks
  • We propose Localized Spectral Convolution (Maintains equivariance, Captures local features)
Non-Linearity and Pooling:
Classification and Scaling:
  • Invariance is not desirable for scaling
  • Classification loss should not increase at a higher scale
  • We propose scale consistency loss

Results

Qualitative Results: Perfect scale equivalence with non-linearity and pooling (Left). Robust to non-ideal down-sampling (Right).

Ideal Downsampling
Non-Ideal Downsampling

Quantitative Results:

Results on MNIST-scale
Results on MNIST-scale with missing Scales

output:
A View From Somewhere: Human-Centric Face Representations

Jerone T. A. Andrews, Przemyslaw Joniak, Alice Xiang

Sony AI, Tokyo; University of Tokyo, Tokyo; Sony AI, New York
jerone.andrews@sony.com
github.com/sony/fax-dataset

Motivation

Dataset Bias: Dataset bias can result in models that are discriminatory or depend on spurious correlations. For instance, a face image restoration model may "hallucinate" features correlated with oversampled subgroups, resulting in the erasure of minority groups.

Prior to learning, human-centric image dataset users should evaluate the diversity of the data.

Parity and Diversity

The canonical approach is to categorize people using demographic labels. However, evaluating diversity by examining counts across subgroups fails to reflect the continuous nature of human phenotypic diversity.

Unknown Label Distributions

Most human-centric image datasets are web-scraped, lacking ground-truth information about the image subjects. Observational labels risk not only encoding stereotypes, but reifying and propagating them beyond their "cultural context".

Contributions

Motivated by issues inherent to categorical labels, we aim to develop a tool that can evaluate the visual diversity of faces in unlabeled datasets. We do so without ever asking an annotator to explicitly categorize a person.

Face Similarity (FAX) Dataset

We introduce a novel dataset, FAX, containing 638,180 odd-one-out face similarity judgments over 4,921 faces. Judgments correspond to the odd-one-out (i.e., least similar) face in a triplet.

Model of Conditional Decision-Making

We introduce a model that learns to predict human judgments of face similarity conditioned on the annotator who generated the judgment.

A face can exhibit certain characteristics to a greater or lesser extent than others, even within the same subpopulation. We constrain our model to learn face embeddings that are continuous, non-negative (human-interpretable), and sparse.

Results

Interpretability: From learning on FAX, we note the materialization of distinct dimensions coinciding with commonly defined demographic subgroups.

Predicting Novel Similarity Judgments

Annotator Bias

Comparative Diversity Auditing

FAX dimensions result in the identification of data subsets with competitive attribute disparity scores.

A Theoretical Analysis of the Test Error of Finite-Rank Kernel Ridge Regression

Tin Sum Cheng, Aurelien Lucchi, Ivan Dokmanić, Anastasios Kratsios, David Belius

University of Basel
{tinsum.cheng, aurelien.lucchi, ivan.dokmanic}@unibas.ch, kratsioa@mcmaster.ca, david.belius@cantab.ch
github.com/tinsumcheng/finite-rank-krr

Contribution

  • 1. Precise non-asymptotic upper and lower bounds for test error;
  • 2. with ridge λ decoupled from sample size N;
  • 3. including inconsistent case (target function outside RKHS) for under-parametrized kernel ridge regression.

Kernel Ridge Regression

A (finite) rank-M kernel K is a map K: X x X -> R defined for any x, x' in X by K(x, x') = sum_{k=1}^M lambda_k psi_k(x) psi_k(x').

Fix a ridge lambda > 0, the regressor f_z,lambda of the finite-rank kernel K is the (unique) minimizer of the empirical risk functional.

Test Error

Note that {psi_k}_{k=1}^M are orthonormal functions. Write target function f* = sum_{k=1}^M gamma_k psi_k + f_perp.

The finite-rank KRR test error is decomposed into Bias-Variance: R_z,lambda = bias + variance.

Where bias measures the error when there is no noise in the label, and variance is defined to be the difference between test error and bias.

References

[1] Mehryar Mohri, Afshin Rostamizadeh, and Ameet Talwalkar. Foundations of machine learning. MIT press (2018).

[2] Arash Amini, Richard Baumgartner, and Dai Feng. Target alignment in truncated kernel ridge regression. NeurIPS (2022).

[3] Francis Bach. Learning theory from first principles. https://www.di.ens.fr/~fbach/ltfp_book.pdf (2023).

Comparison

Main Result

Under mild conditions on the kernel K, for N sufficiently large, there exist constants C1, C2 independent of N and lambda such that with high probability, we have bounds on the test error R_z,lambda.

Experiment

Our bounds are sharp and capture the decay of the test error in N. The figure below shows the simulation on two different kernels.

UniMax: Fairer and More Effective Language Sampling for Large-Scale Multilingual Pretraining

Hyung Won Chung, Xavier Garcia, Adam Roberts, Yi Tay, Orhan Firat, Sharan Narang, Noah Constant

Google Research
h.w.chung27@gmail.com, {nconstant, xgarcia}@google.com

(a) Number of training epochs for each language. Temperature sampling results in a large number of data repeats for low-resource languages, whereas UniMax explicitly caps repeats.

(b) Pretraining sampling distribution. Temperature sampling results in poorly balanced distributions, whereas UniMax provides more uniform distributions without excessive upsampling.

Sampling Strategy

Standard temperature sampling: $p_l \propto n_l^{1/\tau}$.
UniMax sampling: $q_l = \frac{p'_l}{\sum_{l' \in L} p'_{l'}}$ where $p'_l$ is capped.

Algorithm 1: UniMax

Algorithm 1: UNIMAX Inputs : Character count $c_l$ of each language $l$ in all the languages $L$ of the training corpus Total character budget $C$ The number of epochs per language $N$ Output: Sampling distribution $p_l$ of each language // Sort the languages by increasing number of character counts $L \leftarrow sortByCount(L)$ $B \leftarrow C$ // Initialize the remaining budget to the total character budget $i \leftarrow 0$ for $l$ in $L$ do $b_l \leftarrow \frac{B}{len(L)-i}$ // Compute the remaining budget per-language if $b_l > c_l \times N$ then // If per-language budget exceeds $N$ epochs of $l$, use $N$ epochs $U_l \leftarrow c_l \times N$ else $U_l \leftarrow b_l$ // Otherwise use uniform per-language budget end if $B \leftarrow B - U_l$ // Update the remaining budget $i \leftarrow i + 1$ end for $p \leftarrow normalize(U)$ return $p$

Training Dynamics

Figure 2: Pretraining cross-entropy loss on the held-out data over the training steps. With too-low temperature, low-resource languages are sampled too little, and their losses are relatively high. With higher temperature, overfitting becomes more severe with increasing model size.

Downstream Performance (TyDi QA)

Figure 4: Average TyDi QA GoldP performance across three model sizes. Overall, UNIMAX outperforms both baselines at all model sizes considered. Breakdowns on higher-resource (top-5) and lower-resource (bottom-4) languages show UNIMAX outperforms tau=3.33 on both high- and low-resource, and only underperforms tau=1 on high-resource at large model scales.

Long-term Training (1M Steps)

Figure 3: Pretraining cross-entropy loss on the held-out data over 1M training steps. With the sequence length of 512, this corresponds to 1/2 character budget. The overfitting behavior emerges only after sufficient number of training steps for tau=3.33.

Main Results

Table 3: Comparison to mT5. XNLI and PAWS-X show average per-language accuracy; the rest show average per-language EM/F1. We use the translate-train setting except for TyDi QA.

Resources

  • mC4 3.1.0, available via HuggingFace
  • umT5 checkpoints, available via T5X repo

UCL
DeepMind
github.com/deepmind/neupl

NeuPL: Neural Population Learning

Siqi Liu, Luke Marris, Daniel Hennes, Josh Merel, Nicolas Heess, Thore Graepel

Background

+ =
Transitive Skills
Strategic Cycles
Game-of-Skills

The policy space of symmetric zero-sum Game-of-Skills is composed of transitive skill dimensions and intransitive strategic cycles.

Solving intransitive strategy games: Game-theoretic population learning algorithms (e.g. FP, PSRO) offer convergence guarantees to an NE, where each policy best-responds to a mixture over predecessors.

Fictitious Play
PSRO-NASH

However, when combined with modern deep RL techniques, population learning comes at significant costs, for two reasons:

  • Lack of positive transfer across policies;
  • Suboptimal "good"-responses populating the policy population;

Can we leverage the expressiveness of modern deep NNs to enable a single model to explore, represent and reason about diverse strategies while retaining game theoretic guarantees using the same infrastructure as "self-play"?

Methods

Algorithm 1 Neural Population Learning (Ours)
1: Πθ(·|s, σ) ▷ Conditional neural population net.
2: Σ := {σi}i=1N ▷ Initial interaction graph.
3: F : RN×N → RN×N ▷ Meta-graph solver.
4: while true do
5: ΠθΣ ← {Πθ(·|s, σi)}i=1N ▷ Neural population.
6: for σi ∈ UNIQUE(Σ) do
7: Πθσi ← Πθ(·|s, σi)
8: Πθσi ← ABR(Πθσi, σi, ΠθΣ) ▷ Self-play.
9: U ← EVAL(ΠθΣ) ▷ (Optional) if F adaptive.
10: Σ ← F(U) ▷ (Optional) if F adaptive.
11: return Πθ, Σ

Experiments (RwS)

Forward Transfer

Improved Population Learning

Evaluating a NeuPL population at different steps against fixed PSRO policy populations obtained after 8 iterations.

References

[1] Czarnecki, Wojciech M., et al. "Real World Games Look Like Spinning Tops." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 33 (2020).
[2] Balduzzi, David, et al. "Open-ended learning in symmetric zero-sum games." International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2019.
[3] Lanctot, Marc, et al. "A unified game-theoretic approach to multiagent reinforcement learning." Advances in neural information processing systems 30 (2017).
[4] Vinyals, Oriol, et al. "Grandmaster level in StarCraft II using multi-agent reinforcement learning." Nature 575.7782 (2019): 350-354.

Inverse Approximation Theory for Nonlinear Recurrent Neural Networks

Shida Wang1, Zhong Li2 and Qianxiao Li1

1Department of Mathematics, National University of Singapore
2Microsoft Research Asia

Abstract

We prove an inverse approximation theorem for the approximation of nonlinear sequence-to-sequence relationships using recurrent neural networks (RNNs). This is a so-called Bernstein-type result in approximation theory, which deduces properties of a target function under the assumption that it can be effectively approximated by a hypothesis space. In particular, we show that nonlinear sequence relationships that can be stably approximated by nonlinear RNNs must have an exponential decaying memory structure - a notion that can be made precise.

Recurrent neural networks

Let m be the hidden dimension. With trainable weights c, W, U and activation σ, hk+1 = σ(Whk + Uxk), h0 = 0 (1) yk = cThk (2) Here x are the inputs and y are the outputs.

Stable approximation

Definition 1 (Stable approximation via parameterized models). Let β0 > 0. We say a target functional sequence H admits a β0-stable approximation under {H(m)}, if there exists a sequence of parameterized approximants Hm = H(·, θm), θm ∈ Θm which are continuous in input x in point-wise topology.

The following two graphs are perturbation errors for exponential decaying memory and polynomial decaying memory.

Memory function

Definition 2 (Memory function of nonlinear functional sequences). For continuous, causal, regular and time-homogeneous functional sequences H, define the memory function of H over bounded Heaviside input uτ.

This memory function definition is a natural extension for the function ρ in linear functional. For continuous, causal, regular and time-homogeneous functional sequences H, we say it has a decaying memory if the limit of the memory function goes to zero as t approaches infinity.

Main theorems

Theorem 3 (Approximation Theorem - Curse of memory in nonlinear RNNs). Assume H is a sequence of continuous, causal, regular and time-homogeneous functionals on X with decaying memory. Suppose there exists a sequence of hardtanh RNNs stably approximating H. Then, the memory function of the nonlinear functional decays exponentially.

Curse of memory phenomenon in nonlinear RNNs

For nonlinear functionals with polynomial decaying memory function and tanh RNNs:

Reparameterization enables stable approximation

For linear RNNs, stable approximation of target functionals with polynomial decaying memory can be achieved via reparameterization of the recurrent weight matrix W. Exponential parameterization W = -eM and softplus parameterization W = -log(eM + I) are shown:

Conclusion

We derive the first known Bernstein-type result in the setting of sequence modelling using nonlinear RNNs. We show that, assuming a given target sequence relationship can be stably approximated by RNNs with hardtanh/tanh-like activations, then the target functional sequence's memory structure must be exponentially decreasing.

References

  • Shida Wang, Zhong Li, and Qianxiao Li. "Inverse Approximation Theory for Nonlinear Recurrent Neural Networks." In The Twelfth International Conference on Learning Representations. 2023.
  • Haotian Jiang, Qianxiao Li, Zhong Li, and Shida Wang. (2023). A Brief Survey on the Approximation Theory for Sequence Modelling. Journal of Machine Learning. 2 (1). 1-30. doi:10.4208/jml.221221
  • Wang, Shida, and Qianxiao Li. "StableSSM: Alleviating the Curse of Memory in State-Space Models Through Stable Reparameterization." arXiv preprint arXiv:2311.14495 (2023).

Conformal Semantic Keypoint Detection with Statistical Guarantees

Heng Yang, Marco Pavone

Workshop on Robot Learning: Trustworthy Robotics

Object Pose Estimation: why untrustworthy?

Current pipelines rely on heuristic prediction which are not guaranteed to be close to groundtruth. Popular heuristics (e.g., RANSAC) get trapped in local minima and fail without notice.

Our method: conformal keypoint detection

Paradigm shift: from heuristic prediction to probabilistically correct prediction sets.

Key ingredient: inductive conformal prediction (ICP) — finite-sample uncertainty quantification without assumptions on the data distribution or the prediction model.

  • Requirement #1: Calibration set
  • Requirement #2: Nonconformity function
  • Guarantee: Cover the true keypoints with a user-specified probability (e.g., 90%)

Experiments: works out of the box!

Setup: 200 images for calibration, 1214 images for testing.

User: give me 90% and 95% coverage of the true keypoints.

The results demonstrate valid coverage (90%, 95%) and small size of the prediction sets across various objects in the LineMOD Occluded dataset.

DeepJoint: Robust Survival Modelling Under Clinical Presence Shift

Vincent Jeanselme¹, Glen Martin², Matthew Sperrin², Niels Peek², Brian Tom¹ and Jessica Barrett¹

¹ MRC Biostatistics Unit, University of Cambridge
² Health e-Research Centre, University of Manchester
github.com/vjeanselme/DeepJoint

Preprint

Code

Motivation

  • Observations' times and types reflect the patient's condition and the clinical staff's assessment of what was needed and when.
  • Predictive models have either partially leveraged this clinical presence process for improved performance or assumed it uninformative.
  • Although practitioners justify ignoring clinical presence to avoid leveraging a changeable and unpredictable process, no work has explored the impact of handling clinical presence on robustness.
  • Our work explores (i) how state-of-the-art methodologies behave under clinical presence shifts, and (ii) how to leverage clinical presence for improved transportability.

Methodology

We study in-ICU mortality prediction in MIMIC III leveraging laboratory tests after a 24-hour observation period.

Motivated by the difference in the counts of tests (Fig. 3) performed on patients admitted on weekends and weekdays, we hypothesise that practice might differ between weekends and weekdays.

  • Last - DeepSurv multi-layer perceptron relying on the last observation.
  • Count - Concatenate the 24-hour count of each observation to the last observation.
  • Ignore - An LSTM leveraging the data while ignoring its irregularity.
  • Resample - An LSTM with regularly resampled data.
  • GRU-D - Data concatenated with missingness indicators as inputs to a GRU-D.
  • Feature - An LSTM leveraging missingness and time elapsed since previous observation as inputs.
  • DeepJoint - The proposed methodology (Fig. 1) leveraging the same input as Feature.

Figure 1: DeepJoint - Joint modelling of clinical presence and survival outcome. An LSTM network extracts an embedding h which is leveraged to model the clinical presence mechanisms and the survival outcome.

Figure 3: Difference in tests ordered stratified by admission day and outcome.

Figure 4: Robustness performance on weekdays admissions. One model is trained on patients admitted on weekend and another model on weekdays. Both models are tested on the same set of weekdays patients.

Results

Figure 4 presents the transportability performance on patients admitted on weekdays measured through time-dependent C-index at three time horizons.

Insight 1 - Missingness and observation times are informative of short-term outcomes.

Insight 2 - Ignoring clinical presence might not only reduce performance but also hurt transportability.

Insight 3 - Explicit modelling of clinical presence improves performance and robustness.

Conclusions

Learning from medical data without sufficient consideration of the potential informativeness of clinical presence on outcome could impair performance and transportability.

We invite practitioners to:

  • Explore the practitioner-patient interaction process to identify clinical presence patterns.
  • Report the assumptions made on the clinical presence process.
  • Leverage informative observation process.
  • Evaluate transportability: internally and externally.
[1] M Van Smeden et al. A cautionary note on the use of the missing indicator method for handling missing data in prediction research, 2020
[2] A Johnson et al. Mimic-III, a freely accessible critical care database, 2016
[3] Z Che et al. Recurrent neural networks for multivariate time series with missing values, 2018
Work supported by the UKRI Medical Research Council (MC UU 00002/5 & MC UU 00002/2).

output:
Spatial Graph Attention and Curiosity-driven Policy for Antiviral Drug Discovery

Yulun Wu, Mikaela Cashman, Nicholas Choma, Erica T. Prates, Verónica Melesse Vergara, Andrew Chen, Manesh Shah, Austin Clyde, Thomas S. Brettin, Wibe A. de Jong, Neeraj Kumar, Martha S. Head, Rick L. Stevens, Peter Nugent, Daniel A. Jacobson, James B. Brown

University of California, Berkeley
Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
github.com/berkeleylab/dgapn

Overview

Distilled Graph Attention Policy Network (DGAPN) is a reinforcement learning model for generating novel graph-structured chemical representations that optimize user-defined objectives by efficiently navigating a constrained domain.

The framework is examined on the task of generating molecules that are designed to bind, noncovalently, to functional sites of a SARS-CoV-2 protein.

Our proposed method:

  • uses state-of-the-art policy gradient reinforcement learning framework
  • defines fragment-interchanging-based action space that is chemically reasonable
  • embeds both atom and bond attributes as well as spatial structures by leveraging self-attention
  • incorporates an efficient surrogate approach that proposes curiosity bonus to enhance exploration
  • is parallelized in various fashions to fit specific needs

Distilled Graph Attention Policy Network

The generating process is framed as a Markov decision problem. In a single step, the CReM environment proposes candidates that are reasonable evolutions for the current molecular state; the current state and candidates are encoded into hidden graph representations by a shared Spatial Graph Attention (sGAT) network, then encoded into vector representations by two separate networks; a retrieval system attention mechanism is applied to predict the selection probability of each candidate.

Reward from Protein-Ligand Structural Information

The main reward of a generated ligand is its binding affinity estimate against the catalytic site of SARS-CoV-2 protein NSP15, predicted by molecular docking.

Experiments: Single-objective optimization

The negative docking score is used as the main reward and assign it to the final state as the single objective.

Experiments: Constrained optimization

In lead optimization problems in drug discovery and material science, it can be useful to require the generated molecule to maintain a certain level of similarity to its starting molecule.

Multi-objective optimization

The docking reward is weighted with a formula including QED and SA.

Conclusions

Our framework advances the state-of-the-art algorithms in the optimization of molecules with antiviral potential while maintaining reasonable synthetic accessibility. A valuable extension of our work would be to focus on lead-optimization — the refinement of molecular fragments already known to bind the protein of interest through position-constrained modification.

References

Burda, Yuri, et al. "Exploration by random network distillation." arXiv:1810.12894 (2018).
Schulman, John, et al. "Proximal policy optimization algorithms." arXiv:1707.06347 (2017).
Bickerton, G. Richard, et al. "Quantifying the chemical beauty of drugs." Nature chemistry 4.2 (2012).
†University of California, Berkeley, ‡Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, §National Virtual Biotechnology Laboratory

Extending Kernel PCA through Dualization: Sparsity, Robustness and Fast Algorithms

Francesco Tonin, Alex Lambert, Panagiotis Patrinos, Johan Suykens

ESAT, KU Leuven, Belgium

Kernel PCA Problem

Given: n datapoints (xi) in X, feature map φ: X → H to a Hilbert space H.

Goal: find s directions in H that maximize the variance under orthonormal conditions. The KPCA optimization problem is

sup ||ΓW||²_F / 2

We use the following definitions:

  • The Stiefel manifold of orthonormal s-frames in H.
  • G(W) is the matrix such that G(W)ij = (wi, wj).
  • Γ: H^s → R^{n×s} is the linear operator s.t. for all (i, j).
  • G is the Gram matrix G = [k(xi, xj)], where k is the positive definite kernel function.

The usual way to solve (1) is through SVD of G, which is slow with larger n.

Paper TL;DR
We propose a duality framework to solve the KPCA problem faster, with extension to robust and sparse losses.

Difference of convex functions

Key idea: Rewrite (1) as a difference of convex functions

inf g(W) - f(ΓW)

Two key advantages:

  • Allows new gradient-based algorithm to solve KPCA efficiently without the SVD of G.
  • It becomes possible to slightly modify the loss function f to enforce specific properties such as robustness or sparsity.

Proposition 0.1 (Dual of difference of convex functions). Let U, K be two Hilbert spaces, g: U → R and f: K → R be two convex lower semi-continuous functions and Γ ∈ L(U, K).

Faster KPCA with Gradient Descent

Motivation for going from primal to dual: we show that g*(Γ^T H) is related to the nuclear norm of some low dimensional matrix.

Proposition 0.2. Let g be the indicator function of the Stiefel manifold and Γ as in Problem 1. Then for all H ∈ R^{n×s}:

g*(Γ^T H) = Tr √H^T G H

The computational complexity of computing the gradient of π:

  • Computation of H^T G H in O(sn²).
  • SVD of H^T G H in O(s³).

KPCA Training Time for multiple KPCA problems with fixed δ = 10⁻² accuracy. Speedup factor w.r.t. RSVD.

• Influence of the number of components s on training time: higher s leads to longer training times.

Beyond variance maximization

Typical loss function: square loss f = 1/2 ||·||²_F.

Problems: sensible to outliers, no sparsity.

Key idea: use a loss obtained with infimal convolution

f = 1/2 ||·||²_F □ Ψ

where Ψ is a well-chosen function that enforces robustness or sparsity.

DC Optimization

As f is a Moreau envelope, its gradient is always defined for all Y ∈ R^{n×s}.

∇(1/2 ||·||² □ Ψ)(Y) = Y - prox_Ψ(Y)
Algorithm 1 DCA for Moreau envelope objectives
input: Gram matrix G
for epoch t from 0 to T - 1 do
  // alternating gradient steps
  Y = ∇π(H^{(t)})
  H^{(t+1)} = prox_{Ψ*}(Y)
return H^{(T)}

Robustness and sparsity

Denoting ||·||_* as the dual norm of ||·|| and the balls of radius t for these norms as B*_t and B_t, we extend KPCA with Huber and ε-insensitive objectives.

Extended Huber loss H_κ:

Ψ := κ ||·||, Ψ* = ι_{B*_κ}, prox_{Ψ*}(Y) = Proj_{B*_κ}(Y)

• Effect of κ for the losses on contaminated Iris dataset.

Extended ε-insensitive loss l_ε:

Ψ := ι_{B_ε}, Ψ* = ε ||·||_*, prox_{Ψ*}(Y) = Y - Proj_{B_ε}(Y)

• Reconstruction error for the l_∞ loss for multiple ε and s.

PyTorch code

Data-efficient Large Vision Models through Sequential Autoregression

Zhiwei Hao*1, Jianyuan Guo*2, Chengcheng Wang*3, Yehui Tang3, Han Wu2, Han Hu1, Kai Han3, Chang Xu2

*Equal contribution

1School of information and Electronics, Beijing Institute of Technology.
2School of Computer Science, Faculty of Engineering, The University of Sydney.
3Huawei Noah's Ark Lab.
github.com/huawei-noah/Efficient-LVM

Introduction

Training general-purpose large multimodal foundation models on purely sequential data, similar to linguistic inputs, has heralded a new frontier in visual perception. However, current endeavors are hamstrung by an over-reliance on colossal models, exemplified by the necessity for an extensive corpus of data, often comprising staggering 400B tokens. In this work, we delve into the development of an efficient, autoregression-based foundation model, innovatively architected to operate on a limited dataset.

Part-1: Data-efficient Training: Data Augmentation Strategy

Dataset augmentation yielded the best overall performance.

Part-2: Low Power Consumption & Tiny Model: Knowledge Distillation

The introduction of KD proves beneficial in enhancing the single-task performance of LVMs.

The introduction of KD proves beneficial in enhancing the multi-task performance of LVMs.

Beyond Perception: Generation Ability

Next frame prediction: zoom in, zoom out, camera motion.

Image completion.

Sparse Parameterization for Epitomic Dataset Distillation

Xing Wei, Anjia Cao, Funing Yang, Zhiheng Ma*

School of Software Engineering, Xi'an Jiaotong University
Shenzhen Institute of Advanced Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences
github.com/XJTU-XingWei/SPEED

Dataset distillation

Dataset distillation aims to obtain smaller synthetic datasets that capture the essential information of the originals.

The success of dataset distillation relies on two essential factors:

  • Proper parameterization of the synthetic dataset
  • Effective design of the matching objective to align it with the original

Previous explorations

Spatial redundancy exists within individual synthetic images and between different synthetic images.

  • Naive image-independent parameterization: each learnable parameter basis (synthetic image) is optimized independently
  • Recent efficient parameterization: either only consider the connections between synthetic images, or do not reduce the redundancy inside the synthetic image thoroughly

Sparse parameterization

Our sparse parameterization strategy leverages principles from representation learning paradigms, including convolutional neural network, vision transformer, dictionary learning, and sparse coding.

  • Spatial-agnostic epitomic tokens (SAETs) E are shared among all patches in all synthetic images, reducing spatial redundancy
  • Sparse coding matrices (SCMs) Ai are stored in a sparse manner, eliminating inefficient parameters and bringing better generalization
  • Feature-recurrent network (FReeNet) Φθ recurrently use the sparsely combined features, alleviating storage burden

Overview

Storage analysis

  • Coordinate sparse matrix storage format (COO) for SCMs
  • Storage equation under IPC=n storage budget

Comparisons on standard benchmarks

Synthetic images

Application in continual learning

Generalization of synthetic dataset

Neural Tangent Kernels for Axis-Aligned Tree Ensembles

Ryuichi Kanoh, Mahito Sugiyama

National Institute of Informatics, The Graduate University for Advanced Studies, SOKENDAI
github.com/kanoh-ryuichi/ntk-tree

Contribution

  • We formulate the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) induced by infinitely many axis-aligned differentiable tree ensembles.

Differentiable Decision Tree

By employing a sigmoid-like function, we can relax the splitting into a continuous form, which allows the use of gradient descent.

Neural Tangent Kernel for Tree Ensembles

The NTK emerges in the context of functional gradient descent.

  • It does not change during training when considering infinitely wide neural networks.
  • Then, functional behavior becomes analytically tractable.
  • By handling the number of trees like the width of neural networks, we can apply NTK theory to differentiable tree ensembles.
  • In this work, we extend the NTK concept for axis-aligned trees.

Axis-Aligned Constraint

We consider two axis-aligned cases: AAA and AAI.

Closed-form NTK formula for Axis-Aligned Trees

Training behavior is now analytically tractable using our formula.

Ensembles of Various Trees

The NTK induced by an ensemble of various tree architectures is equal to the sum of the NTKs induced by each architecture.

Insight on Oblivious Trees

Any non-oblivious trees can be transformed into oblivious trees that induce exactly the same limiting NTK.

Multiple Kernel Learning (MKL) as Architecture Search

MKL enables tree architecture search based on convex optimization.

MKL weights obtained by the tic-tac-toe endgame dataset

Interactions for tic-tac-toe endgame

Improving Equilibrium Propagation Without Weight Symmetry Through Jacobian Homeostasis

Axel Laborieux, Friedemann Zenke

Friedrich Miescher Institute for Biomedical Research
Faculty of Natural Sciences, University of Basel
ICLR 2023

Generalized Equilibrium Propagation [1]

Goal: Local gradients for Neurobiology and Neuromorphics.

Non-symmetric

Entangled sources of bias:

  • Finite differences ❌
  • Jacobian asymmetry ❌

Our contributions:

  • Disentangle the two sources of bias in non-symmetric EP
  • Extend holomorphic EP [2] to non symmetric networks
  • New homeostatic loss promotes Jacobian symmetry

Accurate computation of the EP error vector

Continuous-in-time estimation of the gradient

The gradient estimate can be computed in continuous time without discrete forward/backward phases ✅

Isolated effect of Jacobian asymmetry

New homeostatic loss:

L_homeo := E[||Jf ε||^2 - ε^T Jf^2 ε]

Minimize sensitivity to perturbation
Increase second-order reconstruction

Benefits of the homeostatic loss on learning

Homeostatic loss is:

  • More general than symmetrising weights ✅
  • Reduces the gap with the energy-based case ✅

References

[1] Scellier, et al. Generalization of equilibrium propagation to vector field dynamics. arXiv:1808.04873, 2018.
[2] Laborieux and Zenke. Holomorphic equilibrium propagation computes exact gradients through finite size oscillations. NeurIPS 2022.

Funding

Paper

output:
Agnostic Learnability of Halfspaces via Logistic Loss

Ziwei Ji (UIUC), Kwangjun Ahn (MIT), Pranjal Awasthi (Google Research), Satyen Kale (Google Research), Stefani Karp (Google Research & CMU)

University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign
Massachusetts Institute of Technology
Google Research
Carnegie Mellon University

Setting.

Binary classification: given by distribution P on Rd × {-1, +1}; we have i.i.d. samples from P.

Linear classifier: x ↦ ⟨x, w⟩.

Goal: compete with the optimal solution ū, where

R0-1(ū) := Pr(x,y)~P (sign(⟨ū, x⟩) ≠ y) = OPT > 0.

Logistic regression (LR): let ℓlog(z) := ln(1 + e-z), and let

Rlog(w) := E(x,y)~P [ℓlog(y⟨w, x⟩)]

denote the population logistic risk of w over P.

Contributions.

Prior lower and upper bounds for LR do not match:
  • General P: 1 - OPT lower bound (Ben-David et al., 2012).
  • Isotropic log-concave distributions: Ω(OPT) lower bound (Diakonikolas et al., 2020).
  • "Well-behaved" sub-exponential distributions: O(√OPT) upper bound with SGD (Frei et al., 2021).
On "well-behaved" conditions:
  • Standard concentration and anti-concentration conditions.
  • Example: a mixture of log-concave dist. (e.g., Gaussian).
Our lower and upper bounds for LR:
  • Ω(√OPT) lower bound for well-behaved sub-exp dist.; matching O(√OPT) upper bound from (Frei et al., 2021).
  • O(OPT) upper bound with "radial Lipschitzness."
Upper bounds beyond LR:
  • Diakonikolas et al. (2020) designed a nonconvex SGD that achieves O(OPT) + ε risk using O(d/ε4) samples.
  • Other prior algorithms achieving O(OPT) + ε risk involve solving multiple rounds of convex program.
  • We design a simple two-phase convex program that achieves O(OPT ln(1/OPT)) + ε risk using O(d/ε2) samples, without radial Lipschitzness.

Lower bound.

We construct a distribution Q with three parts Q1, Q2, Q4.

  • 1. Q1 consists of two squares with edge length √OPT/2 and density 1; one has center (√2/2, -√2/2) and label -1, the other has center (-√2/2, √2/2) and label +1.
  • 2. Q2 is supported on [0, √OPT] × [0, 1] and [-√OPT, 0] × [-1, 0] with density 1, and the label is given by sign(x1).
  • 3. Q4 is the uniform distribution over the unit ball with density q4 and label sign(x1).

Theorem. Q is well-behaved and sub-exponential, and the global minimizer w* of Rlog has R0-1(w*) = Ω(√OPT).

Proof ideas:
  • To minimize Rlog on Q4, we should go to infinity along (1, 0).
  • Q1 keeps w* bounded with ||w*|| = O(1/√OPT).
  • Q2 drags w* above by an angle of Ω(√OPT).

Upper bound: radial Lipschitzness.

To exclude Q and overcome the lower bound, we make the following assumption for LR.

Radial Lipschitzness: There exists a measurable function κ : R+ → R+ such that for any two-dimensional subspace V, letting pV denote the density of the projection of feature distribution onto V, then

|pV(r, θ) - pV(r, θ')| ≤ κ(r)|θ - θ'|.
  • Holds if pV is Lipschitz, even non-log-concave ones (e.g., Gaussian mixtures).
  • Does not hold for general log-concave distributions.
  • Does not hold for Q, non-Lipschitz near the vertical axis.

Upper bound: logistic regression.

Projected gradient descent (PGD): let w0 := 0, and

wt+1 := ΠB(1/√ε) [wt - η∇R̂log(wt)].

Theorem. If the dist. is well-behaved, sub-exponential and radially-Lipschitz, with learning rate Õ(1/d), using poly(d, 1/ε, ln(1/δ)) samples and iterations, with prob. 1 - δ, PGD outputs wt with

R0-1(wt) = Õ(OPT) + ε.

Proof ideas: under the given conditions, suppose ŵ satisfies Rlog(ŵ) ≤ Rlog(||w̄||ū) + ε', then

R0-1(ŵ) = Õ ( max { OPT, √(ε' / ||w̄||) · (Cκ / ||w̄||2) } ).
  • Cκ = O(ln(1/OPT)2) for Lipschitz density.
  • The global optimizer w* of Rlog satisfies ||w*|| = Ω(1/√OPT); PGD also ensures wt has small ε' and ||wt|| = Ω(1/√OPT).

Upper bound: two-phase convex program.

Motivation: (1) holds for the hinge loss ℓh(z) := max{-z, 0} without radial Lipschitzness!

R0-1(ŵ) = Õ ( max { OPT, √(ε' / ||w̄||) } ).

However, the global minimizer of Rh is 0; if we find ŵ by global minimization, as ε' gets small, ||ŵ|| also gets small.

Ideas: LR followed by restricted Perceptron:
  • LR finds unit v that is Õ(√OPT) away from ū.
  • Then minimize Rh over D := {w | ⟨w, v⟩ ≥ 1}.
    • ∀w ∈ D, ||w|| ≥ 1.
    • ||w̄||ū may not in D, but (1 + Õ(OPT))||w̄||ū ∈ D!
  • Use SGD for better sample complexity.

Theorem. If the dist. is well-behaved and sub-exponential, using Õ(d/ε2) samples, SGD can achieve zero-one risk O(OPT ln(1/OPT)) + ε.

output:
Trustworthy Machine Learning ICML 2024

Unveiling Privacy, Memorization, and Input Curvature Links

Deepak Ravikumar, Efstathia Soufleri, Abolfazl Hashemi, Kaushik Roy

Purdue University
github.com/DeepakRavikumar/Privacy-Memorization-Curvature
OVERVIEW

Deep Neural Nets (DNNs) often overfit and memorize training data, impacting generalization, noisy learning, and privacy. Feldman (2019) proposed a formal memorization score, but it is computationally intensive. Recent work links input loss curvature with memorization, showing it to be much more efficient. We theoretically connect memorization, differential privacy, and input loss curvature, validating our findings on CIFAR and ImageNet datasets.

Motivation and Background

Low Input Curvature Typical Easy

Images from ImageNet ranked using input loss curvature. Input loss curvature was obtained using a single ResNet18 trained on ImageNet. Ten lowest curvature samples (top) and ten highest curvature samples (bottom) from the training set are visualized for 5 classes. Low curvature samples are 'prototypical' of their class, while high curvature samples are rare, difficult, and more likely memorized instances.

High Input Curvature Atypical Hard

Theory

Our theoretical framework provides upper bounds in Theorems 5.1, 5.3, and 5.4. These are visualized as links between Differential Privacy, Memorization, and Input Loss Curvature.

Theoretical Results

Input Curvature is given by:

Curv_φ(z_i, S) = tr(H) = tr(∇²_x l(h_θ, z_i))

  • Theorem 5.1 Input Curvature Upper Bounds Memorization
  • Lemma 5.2 Privacy ⇒ Stability
  • Theorem 5.3 Privacy ⇒ Low Input Loss Curvature
  • Theorem 5.4 Privacy ⇒ Less Memorization

Empirical Results

Memorization vs. Input Curvature

→ Theorem 5.1 →

We calculate curvature scores by averaging over many seeds at the end of training. This measurement is proportional to the expected curvature score in Theorem 5.1.

Differential Privacy vs. Input Curvature

→ Theorem 5.3 →

We study the relation between privacy and curvature, we train private ResNet18 models on CIFAR10 and CIFAR100 using DP-SGD. We aim to plot privacy budget vs curvature score and validate Theorem 5.3.

Differential Privacy vs. Memorization

← Theorem 5.4 ←

We how privacy affects memorization for the top 500 most memorized examples. This is done by varying the privacy budget. The results align with Theorem 5.4, showing an increase in memorization as the privacy budget increases.

Summary

  • We explore the theoretical link between memorization, curvature, and privacy.
  • Theoretical analysis is based on assumptions of stability, generalization, and Lipschitzness, applicable to non-convex settings like DNNs.
  • Main result: Memorization is upper-bounded by the curvature of the loss with respect to input and privacy.
  • Privacy bounds input curvature and memorization.
  • Theory validated using DNNs on CIFAR100 and ImageNet, showing a strong match between theoretical predictions and empirical results.

MIA Perspective

A common approach to measure privacy is via membership inference attack (MIA). We used the LiRA (Carlini et al., 2022). More memorized samples are easier to detect using MIA attacks. For ImageNet MIA performance depends on the accuracy of the samples in the trainset.

References

Abadi, M., Chu, A., Goodfellow, I., McMahan, H. B., Mironov, I., Talwar, K., and Zhang, L. Deep learning with differential privacy. In Proceedings of the 2016 ACM SIGSAC conference on computer and communications security, pp. 308-318, 2016.

Feldman, V. Does learning require memorization? a short tale about a long tail. arXiv preprint arXiv:1906.05271, 2019.

Nasr, M., Song, S., Thakurta, A., Papernot, N., and Carlini, N. Adversary instantiation: Lower bounds for differentially private machine learning. In 2021 IEEE Symposium on security and privacy (SP), pp. 866-882. IEEE, 2021.

output:
All in a Row: Compressed Convolution Networks for Graphs

Junshu Sun, Shuihui Wang, Xinzhe Han, Zhe Xue, Qingming Huang

1: Key Laboratory of Intelligent Information Processing, ICT, CAS, China
2: School of Computer Science and Technology, UCAS, China
github.com/ict-vcl/CoCN

Motivation

Compared to Euclidean convolution, existing graph convolution methods generally fail to learn diverse convolution operators under limited parameter scales and depend on additional treatments of multi-scale feature extraction. In this paper, we want to bridge the gap between Euclidean space and graph space, and therefore generalize Euclidean convolution to graphs.

From the Euclidean convolution perspective, it is sensitive to the local spatial order, while GNNs should preserve permutation invariance, i.e., produce the same node representations regardless of the order of the nodes.

Notations

Method

Regularization on Graphs: We propose to regularize graph-structured data through learnable permutations for end-to-end GNNs. Position Regression: Nodes with similar features or short paths in between will get closer position prediction.

Compressed Convolution Network: Learnable permutations decouple the input order and the follow-up module, therefore we can apply either permutation-sensitive or permutation-invariant operators on graphs.

  • Diagonal convolution learns both node features and structure features explicitly.
  • CoCNs directly apply hierarchical feature learning on graphs.
  • CoCNs use anti-diagonal compression to update structure features.

Main Results

Comparison with State-of-the-Arts:

Partial Model Study and Key Conclusions

  • Permutation module can permute input nodes with little information loss.
  • CoCNs learn complementary feature representations under different permutations.
  • CoCNs achieve better performance as the receptive field size increases.
  • Both node features and structure features generally contribute to the model prediction.
Poster presented at ICML 2023 in Hawaii.

Transformers, Parallel Computation, and Logarithmic Depth

Clayton Sanford, Daniel Hsu, Matus Telgarsky

Columbia University
New York University
Spotlight Paper

Motivation

Motivating question: How can transformers be understood as models of parallel computation (e.g. MPC)?

  • Representational powers of depth?
  • Multi-step reasoning capabilities?
  • Comparisons with other models (state-space models, sub-quadratic attention transformers, GNNs)?

Transformer Architecture

  • Self-attention unit: f(X) = softmax(XQKᵀXᵀ)XV
  • Multi-headed attention: L(X) = X + Σ h_i(X)
  • Arbitrary element-wise encoding (MLP + positional encoding)
  • Full transformer: T(X) = (φ_D ∘ L_D ∘ ... ∘ L_1 ∘ φ_0)(X)

Massively Parallel Computation (MPC)

Theoretical model of MapReduce by Karloff, et al '10.

  • N-bit input divided among q machines with local memory s.
  • For round r = 1, ..., R: Each machine performs computation and communicates.

(γ, δ)-MPC protocols have local memory s = O(N^δ) and global memory qs = O(N^{1+γ}).

Conjecture (1-cycle vs 2-cycle; Ghaffari, et al '19)

Any (γ, δ)-MPC protocol with δ ∈ (0, 1) that distinguishes one length-N cycle from two length-N/2 cycles uses R = Ω(log N) rounds.

k-Hop Induction Heads

Induction Heads: Toy task to complete bigram via previous occurrence: IH(baebcabebdea) = b.

  • Occurs naturally in trained transformers; useful primitive for mechanistic interpretability.
  • Two-step logical reasoning task with natural two-layer transformer construction.

k-hop Induction Heads: Iterated variant of IH to capture k-step reasoning.

Theory Result #1: Near-equivalence of Transformers & MPC

Theorem (Transformers simulate MPC)

Any R-round (γ, δ)-MPC protocol can be simulated by a transformer of depth L = O(R) and width m = Õ(N^δ).

Theorem (MPC simulates Transformers)

Any transformer of depth L and width m can be simulated by an MPC protocol with R = O(L) rounds.

Theory Result #2: Efficient Multi-Step Reasoning Representations

Construction: Transformers of depth L = ⌊log₂ k⌋ + 2 and width m = O(1) can solve k-IH.

  • First two layers solve induction heads for each input.
  • Subsequent layers employ "pointer doubling" to chain together solutions.

Theory Result #3: Sub-optimality of Other Architectures

Graph Neural Networks: Solving graph connectivity requires L√m = Õ(N^{1/4}).

State-Space Models: RNN, LSTM, Mamba solutions to k-IH require depth L ≥ k or width m = Ω(N/k⁶).

Sub-Quadratic Attention Transformers: Every kernel-based or masking-based transformer variant that solves k-IH has either depth L ≥ k or near-quadratic computation.

Empirical Result #1: Log-depth k-Hop Induction Heads

Task: Multi-task k-IH for k = 0, ..., 16; sequence length N = 100.

Result: Low error when L ≤ ⌊log₂ k⌋, as predicted by theory.

Empirical Result #2: Interpretability of Learned Models

16-IH attention matrices with colored partial solutions.

Yes. Close correspondence between self-attention matrices and partial solutions to k-IH.

Paper Links

Prior paper [SHT23]
This paper [SHT24]
Graph apps [SFH+24]

CROP: Certifying Robust Policies for Reinforcement Learning through Functional Smoothing

Fan Wu, Linyi Li, Zijian Huang, Yevgeniy Vorobeychik, Ding Zhao, Bo Li

University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign
Washington University in St. Louis
Carnegie Mellon University
github.com/lucas-fan/CROP

Background & Motivation

  • Testing and improving the robustness of safety-critical applications before their massive deployment to real-world is of great importance.
  • Robustness certification for a given learning algorithm is needed to end the repeated game between the attackers and defenders.

Threat Model

Testing-time evasion attack on agent's observation. Adversary can apply l2-bounded perturbations.

Certification Goal

Provide test-time certification for the performance of the trained agent in the perturbed environment.

  • 1. Providing criteria for robustness certification.
  • 2. Proposing certification strategies corresponding to different certification criteria.

Certification Criteria

Criterion 1 (state-level): Per-state action stability. Maximum Perturbation Magnitude ε.

Criterion 2 (trajectory-level): Cumulative reward lower bound. Lower bound of perturbed cumulative reward J.

CROP Framework

Per-State Action Certification (CROP-LoAct): Local randomized smoothing on states. Action-value functional smoothing.

  • Lipschitz continuity of Q w.r.t. the state input.
  • Lower bound of ε calculation based on smoothed policy.

Cumulative Reward Certification

CROP-GRe: Global smoothing on trajectories.

  • Expectation bound via mean smoothing.
  • Percentile bound via percentile smoothing.

CROP-LoRe: Local smoothing + adaptive search

Sequence of certified radii for actions and adaptive search algorithm.

1. Trajectory exploration and expansion: obtain the lower bound for ε (DFS).

2. Perturbation magnitude growth: obtain a new ε (priority queue).

Experiments

Setup: Four RL environments: Freeway, Pong, CartPole, Highway. Nine DQN variants including StdTrain, AdvTrain, etc.

Robustness Certification for Per-State Action

  • GradDQN and RadialRL achieve the highest certified radius on Freeway.
  • Periodic patterns can be observed in Pong.

Robustness Certification for Cumulative Reward

  • Under global smoothing, percentile bound is tighter than expectation bound.
  • The certification is relatively tight compared with the empirical result.
Paper
Leaderboard
Repository

ICLR

StyleMorph: Disentangled 3D-Aware Image Synthesis with a 3D Morphable StyleGan

Eric-Tuan Le*1, Edward Bartrum*1,2, Iasonas Kokkinos1

1University College London, 2Alan Turing Institute
UCL
The Alan Turing Institute
github.com/stylemorph/stylemorph

Motivation

Our objective is to fully disentangle 3D shape, viewpoint, object and background generation on photorealistic synthesis.

Contribution

Photorealistic modelling with a 3D morphable model from 2D image supervision. State-of-the-art disentangled control over shape, viewpoint, object and scene appearance.

  • 1. Introducing Template Object Coordinates (TOCS) as a powerful deformation-equivariant descriptor of 3D shape.
  • 2. Conditioning a 2D style-based DNR with TOCS map for shape and appearance disentanglement.

Comparison with SOTA

Overall Pipeline

Morphable Renderer

A SIREN network learns to offset world points to template space. This allows to express each instance as a deformation of an instance-agnostic template shape.

The TOCS Map captures all and only the 3D geometrical aspects of image formation.

Deferred Neural Renderer

The 2D TOCS map conditions StyleGan2 blocks for the object appearance synthesis. Foreground and background are generated independently and composed for foreground/background disentanglement.

Disentangled control

Deformation equivariance

Single-view reconstruction

Quantitative Baseline

Qualitative Baseline

Limitations - Topology variation

Langevin Autoencoders for Learning Deep Latent Variable Models

Shohei Taniguchi, Yusuke Iwasawa, Wataru Kumagai, Yutaka Matsuo

The University of Tokyo

Motivation

Learning deep latent variable models

Latent variable models: p(x; θ) = ∫ p(x | z; θ)p(z)dz

Gradient over model parameters: ∇θ E_p_data(x)[log p(x; θ)] ≈ 1/n Σ ∇θ log p(x_i, z_i; θ)

  • To learn a latent variable model via gradient ascent, we need to approximate the expectation over the latent posterior p(z | x; θ).
  • Variational inference is a dominant approach but the approximation power is limited.
  • Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) is capable of complex posteriors but is slow due to datapoint-wise iterations.

How can we amortize the cost of datapoint-wise iterations?

(a) Variational inference

(b) Langevin dynamics

Preliminary

Langevin dynamics

Langevin dynamics [2] is a gradient-based MCMC.

z' ~ q(z' | z) = N(z'; z + η∇z log p(x, z; θ), 2ηI)

By repeating this iterative update, the samples asymptotically approach the true posterior p(z | x; θ).

Method

Amortized Langevin dynamics

  • In our amortized Langevin dynamics (ALD), we prepare an encoder that maps the observed data into the latent variable, and run MCMC on its parameter space.
  • Markov chain on the latent space is implicitly performed by collecting the output of the encoder.
  • Our main theorem shows that the ALD is valid as MCMC if the encoder takes the form of: f_z|x(x; Φ) = Φg(x).

Langevin Autoencoder

ALD can be applied to the training of deep latent variable models (DLVMs) by slightly modifying the learning algorithm of traditional autoencoders.

We call this learning algorithm of DLVMs the Langevin autoencoder (LAE).

θ, Φ, ψ ← Initialize parameters

repeat

  x(1), ..., x(n) ~ p_data(x)

  for t = 0, ..., T-1 do

    V_t = -Σ log p(x_i, z_i; θ)

    Φ' ~ q(Φ' | Φ)

  end for

  θ ← θ - η∇θ Σ V_t

until convergence

Results

Toy example

True posterior

Variational approximation

Ours

  • VI fails to capture the multimodality of the true posterior.
  • Our ALD can approximate complex multimodal posteriors in toy examples.

Image density estimation

Likelihood evaluation for test data

Our LAE consistently outperforms VI-based methods such as the VAE and other existing MCMC-based methods.

Future works

  • How can we remove the bias of finite-step MCMC samples?
  • Is it possible to apply the ALD (and LAE) to the state-of-the-art DLVMs, such as NVAE [5]?

References

[1] Diederik P Kingma and Max Welling (2013). "Auto-encoding variational bayes." In: arXiv preprint arXiv:1312.6114.
[2] Radford M Neal (2011). "MCMC using hamiltonian dynamics." In: Handbook of Markov Chain Monte Carlo.
[3] Matthew D Hoffman (2017). "Learning deep latent gaussian models with markov chain monte carlo." In: ICML.
[4] Pierre E Jacob et al. (2020). "Unbiased markov chain monte carlo with couplings." In: JRSSB.
[5] Arash Vahdat and Jan Kautz (2020). "Nvae: A deep hierarchical variational autoencoder." In: NeurIPS.

A Robust Test for the Stationarity Assumption in Sequential Decision Making

Jitao Wang[1]*, Chengchun Shi[2], Zhenke Wu[1]

[1] University of Michigan [2] London School of Economics and Political Science
*jitwang@umich.edu
github.com/jitaow/StationarityTest

Preliminary

Sequential Decision Making

  • Markov Decision Process (MDP): {pt(St+1, Rt | At, St)}t
  • Stationarity assumption: H0: {pj(Sj+1, Rj | Sj, Aj)}0≤j≤T-1 are homogeneous over time j.
  • Offline reinforcement learning (RL): static dataset without further interactions.
  • Additional challenges in modern RL problems:
    • State variable St can be high-dimensional (e.g., 7056 for Atari).
    • Dynamics pt can be highly complex (e.g., autonomous driving).
  • Modern ML methods well-suited for non-linearity and high-dimensionality, however, need careful treatment for proper statistical inference.

Proposed Test

  • Graphical illustration
  • The proposed test is based on sample splitting[1].
I1
I2

Data Split

Maximum-type test statistic:

Γ = maxt, b [ t(T-t)/T2 σ(t, hb)-1 |1/|I2| ∑ ψi | ]

where ψi involves a plug-in term and an augmented term to achieve double robustness.

  • hb is a test function from a rich function class and B is the number of test functions.
  • Nuisance functions are estimated using the data from I1. One can use flexible machine/deep learning tools.
  • Gaussian multiplier bootstrap is used to approximate the limiting distribution and compute the p-value.

Theoretical Property

Double Robustness: Suppose H0 holds. For any h ∈ H and t ∈ (0, T-1), we have E[ψi] = 0. Moreover, the equation is doubly robust, i.e., for any estimated nuisance functions, the equality holds as long as either the transition model or the ratio model is correctly specified.

Size: Suppose B = O((NT)c) for any c > 0 and T = O(T) for any t. Under certain regularity conditions including strict stationarity and geometric ergodicity, the convergence speed of nuisance functions satisfies specific rates, then we have:

P(p ≤ α | H0) = α + o(1)

Simulation

□ Fig. A: Double Robustness.

□ Fig. B: Applicability to online settings.

□ Fig. C: Superiority under high-dimensional settings.

Application to Intern Health Study Data

  • Intern Health Study [2]: Wearable and survey data of 1565 medical interns in the U.S. collected for 26 weeks.
  • The goal is to improve health-related outcomes via mobile-push notifications.
  • We consider the next week's daily average step count as the reward Rt.

References

[1] V. Chernozhukov et al., "Double/debiased machine learning for treatment and structural parameters," The Econometrics Journal, 2018.
[2] T. NeCamp et al., "Assessing real-time moderation for developing adaptive mobile health interventions for medical interns," Journal of Medical Internet Research, 2020.
