output:
FLAb: Benchmarking deep learning methods for antibody fitness prediction

Michael Chungyoun, Jeffrey Ruffolo, Jeffrey Gray

Neural Information Processing Systems
Johns Hopkins University
github.com/graylab/FLAb

1 Background

Antibody therapeutics must have a particular set of fitness properties

Six classes of biophysical data relevant to antibody development are (a) expression, (b) thermostability, (c) immunogenicity, (d) binding affinity, (e) aggregation, and (f) polyreactivity

Do deep learning methods capture properties of therapeutic antibodies?

  • 1. Creating a collection of datasets of antibody therapeutic data
  • 2. Benchmark representative AI model likelihoods for fitness prediction

2 Dataset curation

We curated publicly available antibody fitness datasets

This database includes mutational landscapes of 17 distinct antibody families and a total of 13,384 associated fitness metrics. Each sequence is mapped to at least one fitness label pertaining to the 6 fitness properties.

3 Methods

Pipeline for benchmarking protein language models

For each protein language model, we separately input the antibody heavy and light sequences to return two perplexity scores, and we tabulate the average perplexity between the two sequences. We calculate Spearman's (ρ), Pearson's (r) and Kendall tau's (τ) correlation coefficients between perplexity and fitness.

4 Results

A heat map of model-dataset correlations

Summary of Pearson's correlations. Models generally perform best with thermostability and binding affinity datasets, but most struggle with aggregation propensity and polyreactivity.

Intrinsic biophysical properties are more accurately predicted than extrinsic

Models are more accurate at distinguishing intra-family versus inter-family antibody sets

Parameter size influences performance over architecture and dataset composition

Some models favor evolutionary signal rather than physical fitness

5 Conclusion

FLAb is a living benchmark where we will continuously add models evaluated and available antibody data

No model was top performing across all six fitness classes

output:
Mechanistic Lens on Mode Connectivity

Ekdeep Singh Lubana, Eric J. Bigelow, Robert P. Dick, David Krueger, Hidenori Tanaka

University of Michigan
CBS Harvard University
University of Cambridge
NTT Research
github.com/lubana-ig/mechanistic-connectivity

Connectivity of Minimizers from a Given Dataset

  • Mode Connectivity: Minimizers of modern NNs retrieved using SGD on a dataset can be connected via simple paths of zero loss.

Linear: tθ₁ + (1 - t)θ₂;
Quadratic: t²θ₁ + 2t(1 - t)θₘ + (1 - t)²θ₂.

Motivating Problem: Are Minimizers Retrieved from Different Datasets of a Task Connected?

  • Q1: Are mechanistically dissimilar models, which rely on different input attributes to make predictions, connected?
  • Q2: Can we exploit this connectivity?

Towards a Definition of Mechanistic Similarity

  • We use shared invariances to interventions on the data-generating process to define a notion of Mechanistic Similarity between two models.

Synthetically Inducing Mechanistically Dissimilar Models

  • A data-generating process that synthetically embeds controllable, partially predictive cues in the data.
  • Evaluation: Counterfactuals that break predictivity of label by altering synthetic versus natural attributes.
  • Goal: Analyze connectivity of models that rely vs. ignore spurious attributes.

Result 1: Are Mechanistically Dissimilar Minimizers Connected? Yes!

Proposition. (Mode Connectivity under Mechanistic Dissimilarity.) Assume θ₁, θ₂ are minimizers of the loss on a dataset D and induce mechanistically dissimilar models. Given sufficient over-parameterization, there exists a continuous path along which the minimizers are mode connected.

Result 2: Is Mechanistic Similarity and Simplicity of Connectivity Paths Related? Yes!

Conjecture. (Lack of Linear Connectivity implies Mechanistic Dissimilarity.) If two minimizers θ₁ and θ₂ cannot be linear mode connected, their induced models must be mechanistically dissimilar.

Result 3: Altering Mechanisms via Lack of Linear Connectivity

  • Connectivity-Based Fine-Tuning (CBFT): Find a minimizer that lacks linear connectivity but is invariant to the undesirable attribute.
* ResNet-18 models trained on located cue CIFAR-10 (C) and fine-tuned on data w/o cue (FT) show linear connectivity under small-medium learning rates.

output:
How Do Nonlinear Transformers Acquire Generalization-Guaranteed CoT Ability?

Hongkang Li¹, Meng Wang¹, Songtao Lu², Xiaodong Cui², Pin-Yu Chen²

1: Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute; 2: IBM Research
github.com/research-project-cot

Overview

  • Theoretically characterize how training Transformers can enable chain-of-thought (CoT).
  • Quantitative analysis of how context examples affect CoT performance.
  • Theoretical study of why CoT outperforms ICL.

Background and motivation

CoT augments K-step reasoning examples to the query for generation, i.e., an extension of in-context learning (ICL) by multi-step examples.

  • Existing works [1,2,3]: the expressive power.
  • The problem of why a Transformer can be trained to conduct CoT is less investigated.

Problem formulation

Study learning K-step reasoning tasks. Each task f is a composition of {fᵢ} and outputs {zᵢ} for the input z₀.

Learning model: one-layer Transformer F(P) = Σ Wᵥ p̃ᵢ softmax( (Wₖ p̃ᵢ)ᵀ W_Q p̃_query ), where P is the input prompt.

Training: Following theoretical works [4,5] for ICL, we aim to solve an empirical risk minimization by stochastic gradient descent with squared loss.

CoT Inference: The testing prompt consists of examples and a query. CoT repeats two steps for K steps:

  • Greedy decoding to generate the next step.
  • Add the generation to the end of the prompt.

ICL Inference: The testing examples only contain the input and the final-step output.

[1] Li et al. Disscting Chain-of-Thought. NeurIPS 2023. [2] Feng et al. Towards Revealing the Mystery. NeurIPS 2023. [3] Li et al. Chain of Thought Empowers Transformers. ICLR 2024.

Theoretical results

Data & Tasks: The training and testing data are formulated by TTR/TSR patterns. Consider erroneous testing prompts: reasoning in some examples contains incorrect steps.

Theorem 1 (Convergence): With the number of context examples and iterations linear in Ω(α⁻¹), the population risk is ≤ O(ε).

Theorem 2 (CoT Generalization): To achieve a zero CoT error, the number of context examples for task f is proportional to (α' τᶠ ρᶠ)⁻².

Theorem 3 (Comparison with ICL): The success of ICL requires a dominant fraction of correct input-label examples (Condition 1), but CoT does not.

The mechanism of CoT
Proposition 1: The attention weights are concentrated on prompt columns with the same TSR pattern and step index.

Numerical experiments

  • Condition 1 is crucial for the success of ICL but not for CoT.
  • Training dynamics of CoT: same TSR & step matter.

output:
Biologically-inspired adaptive learning in the Hopfield-network based self-optimization model

Aisha Belhadi

Embodied Cognitive Science Unit, Okinawa Institute of Science and Technology Graduate University
Paper
Join me on Zoom!

Biological learning is different from machine learning in key ways

Biological systems operate with:

  • sparse data
  • lower/efficient energy use
  • minimal/variable training
  • trade-offs driving adaptation

Machine learning operates with:

  • large datasets
  • high energy use
  • extensive training
  • trade-offs causing failure

What biological mechanisms could help enhance ML?

Self-optimization (SO) model

The SO model is a Hopfield network with:

  • updating of weights based on a Hebbian learning rule
  • periodic resets of network states

Able to combine optimization and memory stabilization?

Example: learning rate as a function of energy

1. Weight update rule (α: learning rate; s_i, s_j: states of nodes i and j)

w_ij(t + 1) = w_ij(t) + dw_ij(t),    dw_ij = α s_i s_j

2. Learning rate α as a function of energy E:

α = { a / (ΔE + ε_0) if γ ≠ 1; α_0 if γ = 1 }

SO model results for map-coloring problem. From left to right, E=5, E=8, E=12, E=20 (only the two middle results are valid maps).

Implementing mechanisms used by living systems to adapt to their environment may help solve trade-offs inherent to machine learning.

Translating adaptive biological mechanisms into a Hopfield network

Energy distributions at the end of learning for 1000 runs. A slight difference can be observed between γ=1 (learning rate α constant) and γ=0.1 (variable learning rate), but not obviously significant.

What next?

  • Test further implementations of variable learning rate: as function of weights, trigonometric function
  • Test "forgetting": weight decay, inhibition
  • Applications in continuous learning, creativity, robotics
  • Modeling biological systems

References

W. C. Abraham. Metaplasticity: Tuning synapses and networks for plasticity. Nat Rev Neurosci, 9(5):387–387, 2008-05
H. Kitano. Biological robustness. Nat Rev Genet, 5(11):826–837, 2004-11
R. A. Watson, C. L. Buckley, and R. Mills. Optimization in "self-modeling" complex adaptive systems. Complexity, 16(5):17–26, 2011

Acknowledgments

I would like to sincerely thank Natalya Weber (OIST) for her work on the implementation example and for producing the figures in this poster.

OIST
Okinawa Institute of Science and Technology Graduate University
沖縄科学技術大学院大学

InfinityGAN: Towards Infinite-Pixel Image Synthesis

Chieh Hubert Lin, Hsin-Ying Lee, Yen-Chi Cheng, Sergey Tulyakov, Ming-Hsuan Yang

1UC Merced
2Snap Research
3CMU
4Google Research
5Yonsei University
github.com/hubert0527/infinityGAN
TL;DR We tackle the problem of synthesizing images at arbitrarily sizes, up to infinitely large.

Synthesizing infinite-pixel images from finite-sized training data.

A 1024x2048 image composed of 242 patches, independently synthesized by InfinityGAN with spatial fusion of two styles. The generator is trained on 101x101 patches sampled from 197x197 real images.

Without carefully taming the positional information in the generator, other frameworks cannot generalize to extended image sizes at testing.

InfinityGAN + GAN-inversion (In&Out @ CVPR'22) = Seamless and arbitrarily-length image outpainting / inbetweening

More samples from models trained at higher-resolution settings.

A 256x10240 image ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓

We show that the two sets of latent variables learn different and diverse appearances.

github.com/osil-ddt

Introduction

The vision of Runtime One-shot imitation Learning

Achieve any tasks directly "prompted" by corresponding demonstration.

Popular paradigm: Transformer + behavior cloning

Our focus: Generalization Challenges of runtime OSIL

  • Unseen demonstrations in unseen environments.
  • Unforeseen changes after demonstrations collection.

Methodology

KEY1: Inject the inductive bias of "how human make decisions in runtime OSIL" into the imitator policy network.

An example of 3-stage OSIL of humans.

  • Stage 1: Identify relevant states within the trajectory based on the current state.
  • Stage 2: Analyze the expert's behavior patterns associated with these states.
  • Stage 3: Trace the expert's demonstrations based on the relationship between the current state and the expert's behavior patterns.
KEY2: Solve runtime one-shot imitation learning by context-based meta-RL, instead of supervised learning.
  • The unforeseen changes will randomly appear in the simulators (M).
  • With meta-RL, the imitator policy will try to achieve all of the targets the same to the demonstration guided by 0-1 task rewards.
  • In the process, the imitator policy will suffer from the unforeseen changes and have to handle them before achieve the targets.

Major Experiments

Q1: One-shot imitation ability in unseen situations

Q2: Does DDT imitating via tracing the demos

Q3: The "Scaling Law" of DDT in the OSIL setting

https://osil-ddt.github.io/

DRAGONN: Distributed Randomized Approximate Gradients of Neural Networks

Zhuang Wang*, Zhaozhuo Xu*, Xinyu Crystal Wu, Anshumali Shrivastava, and T. S. Eugene Ng

* equal contribution

Rice University
github.com/rice-networks/DRAGONN

Introduction

Data-parallel Distributed Training (DDT)

Training with multiple GPUs

Communication bottleneck in DDT

Computation gets faster
  • Advanced DNN accelerators (P100 -> V100 -> A100)
  • Advanced DNN compilers (XLA, TVM, etc.)
  • Single-GPU iteration time of ResNet50 reduced by 22x
Network bandwidth can't catch up
  • Slower-growing bandwidth (< 10x increase)
Communication becomes the performance bottleneck

Gradient Sparsification (GS)

Select top-k gradients for synchronization
  • Exact TopK GS
  • Approximate TopK GS, e.g., DGC
Save up to 99.9% the gradient exchange

Greatly reduce the communication time

Method

Limitations of previous GS

GS does not always help
  • Full synchronization is better than DGC for smaller tensors
GS becomes the major bottleneck
  • Compression time exceeds communication time

DRAGONN: a hashing-based compressor

Cheap encoding operations

Deploying DRAGONN in Practice

Efficiency-aware tensor selection for GS
  • Apply DRAGONN to tensors only when it benefits the iteration time
Sparse decoding
  • Minimize the decoding overhead after communication

Experiment

Evaluations

Training throughput
Accuracy vs Iteration
Improvement breakdown

Acknowledgements

NSF, ONR DURIP, ONR BRC and Ken Kennedy Institute BP fellowship

Contributions

  • A generic framework to design a GNN from any fragment of an algebraic language.
  • The instantiation of the framework on ML(L3) resulting in G2N2, a provably 3-WL GNN.
  • Experiments demonstrating that G2N2 outperforms existing 3-WL GNNs on various downstream tasks.

MATLANG and WL

Definition 1: MATLANG is a matrix language with operation set {+, ·, ⊙, T, Tr, diag, 1, ×, f}.

Restricting operations to a subset L defines a fragment of MATLANG ML(L).

Context-Free Grammar

Definition 2: A Context-Free Grammar (CFG) G = (V, Σ, R, S) with a finite set of variables.

Grammar of literature GNNs

GCN: Vc -> CVc | 1
PPGN: M -> MM | diag(1) | A

Input-gradient space particle inference for neural network ensembles

Trung Trinh, Markus Heinonen, Luigi Acerbi, Samuel Kaski

Aalto University, Helsinki University, University of Manchester
github.com/trungtrinh/forde

Overview

TL;DR: We learn an ensemble of neural networks that is diverse with respect to their input gradients.

Repulsive deep ensembles (RDEs) [1]

Description: Train an ensemble using Wasserstein gradient descent, which employs a kernelized repulsion term to diversify the particles.

  • The driving force directs particles towards high density regions.
  • The repulsion force pushes particles away to enforce diversity.

Problem: Weight-space or function-space repulsion often results in underfitting or is ineffective.

Defining the input-gradient kernel

Given a base kernel, we define the kernel in the input-gradient space for a minibatch:

We choose the RBF kernel on a unit sphere:

First-order repulsive deep ensembles (FoRDEs)

Possible advantages:

  • Each member is guaranteed to represent a different function.
  • Issues of weight- and function-space repulsion are avoided.
  • Encourages learning different features for robustness.

Main takeaways

  • 1. Input-gradient-space repulsion can perform better than weight- and function-space.
  • 2. Better corruption robustness can be achieved by configuring the repulsion kernel.

Benchmark comparison

Table 1: FoRDE-PCA achieves the best performance under corruptions on CIFAR-100.

Illustrative experiments

FoRDEs capture higher uncertainty than baselines in regions outside of the training data.

Benchmark comparison (cont.)

Table 2: Results on CIFAR-10 dataset.

[1] F. D'Angelo and V. Fortuin, "Repulsive deep ensembles are Bayesian," NeurIPS 2021.
[2] C. Liu et al., "Understanding and Accelerating Particle-Based Variational Inference," ICML 2019.

Tuning the lengthscales Σ

Each lengthscale is inversely proportional to the strength of the repulsion force.

Proposition: Apply strong forces in high-variance dimensions and weak forces in low-variance dimensions.

Lengthscale tuning experiments

  • Blue lines show accuracies of FoRDEs, dotted orange show Deep Ensembles.
  • FoRDEs exhibit small performance degradations on clean images but become more robust against natural corruptions.

However, recent studies have exposed vulnerabilities in DGL models, such as susceptibility to graph injection attacks (GIAs). Existing research only provides sample-wise certificates by verifying each node independently, leading to very limited certifying performance. In this paper, we present the first collective certificate against GIAs, which certifies a set of target nodes simultaneously."], "tables": [], "figures": []}, {"id": "section_2", "title": "Randomized Smoothing", "content": ["For any node classifier f(·), its smoothed classifier g(·) is created by:", "1. Node-aware Bi-Smoothing (Y. Lai. et. al. S&P2024): φ(G) = (φe(G), φn(G))", "2. For example, in the Citeseer dataset, when ρ = 140, our Collective-LP1 and Collective-LP2 have the certified ratios of 73.0%, and 81.2%, while sample-wise can certify 0.0% nodes."], "tables": [], "figures": []}]}
output:
Collective Certified Robustness Against Graph Injection Attacks

Yuni Lai 1, Bailin Pan 2, Kaihuang Chen 2, Yancheng Yuan 2, Kai Zhou 1

1. Department of Computing, The Hong Kong Polytechnic University
2. Department of Applied Mathematics, The Hong Kong Polytechnic University
github.com/project-placeholder

Introduction

Deep Graph Learning (DGL) has emerged as a crucial technique across various domains. However, recent studies have exposed vulnerabilities in DGL models, such as susceptibility to graph injection attacks (GIAs). Existing research only provides sample-wise certificates by verifying each node independently, leading to very limited certifying performance. In this paper, we present the first collective certificate against GIAs, which certifies a set of target nodes simultaneously.

Randomized Smoothing

For any node classifier f(·), its smoothed classifier g(·) is created by:

  • 1. Node-aware Bi-Smoothing (Y. Lai. et. al. S&P2024): φ(G) = (φe(G), φn(G))
  • 2. Majority vote: gv(G) := arg max P(fv(φ(G)) = y)

The goal of certified robustness is to verify: gv(G) = gv(G'), ∀ G' ∈ Bρ,τ(G).

Collective Certificate Problem

Bρ,τ(G): the attacker can inject ρ malicious nodes, with τ malicious edges per node.

T: A set of target nodes.

min |T| - Σ I{gv(G') ≠ gv(G)}

s.t. |Ṽ| ≤ ρ, δ(ṽ) ≤ τ, ∀ ṽ ∈ Ṽ

Figure 1: While the sample-wise certificate verifies target nodes one by one, our collective certificate verifies a set of target nodes simultaneously by linear programming.

Figure 2: Malicious message has probability to be intercepted in the Node-aware Bi-smoothing.

Let p(Ev) denotes the probability that the malicious messages is NOT intercepted, then we have that the absolute change of prediction probability is bounded by p(Ev):

|pv,y(G) - pv,y(G')| ≤ p(Ev)

Collective Certificate via Optimization

p(Ev): the probability that the malicious messages is not intercepted.

cv := pv,y*(G) - max pv,y(G).

max Σ I{p(Ev) ≥ cv/2}

s.t. |Ṽ| ≤ ρ, δ(ṽ) ≤ τ, ∀ ṽ ∈ Ṽ

The remaining |T| - M* nodes are certified robust.

Sample-wise v.s. Collective Certificate

Sample-wise certificates essentially verify the prediction for a node one by one, assuming that the attacker can craft a different perturbed graph each time to attack every single node (Figure 1, top).

However, in reality, the attacker can only produce a single perturbed graph to simultaneously disrupt all predictions for a set of target nodes (Figure 1, bottom).

Figure 3: Certified ratio comparison (GCN model).

Evaluation Results

With the same smoothing parameter, both proposed collective certificates achieve a higher certifiable radius (Figure 3), outperforming the sample-wise approach significantly when the ρ is large. For example, in the Citeseer dataset, when ρ = 140, our Collective-LP1 and Collective-LP2 have the certified ratios of 73.0%, and 81.2%, while sample-wise can certify 0.0% nodes.

output:
Arizona State University

PatchRot: A Self-Supervised Technique for Training Vision Transformers

Sachin Chhabra, Prabal Bijoy Dutta, Hemanth Venkateswara, Baoxin Li

NeurIPS
github.com/patchrot-project

Problem Statement

  • Vision transformers require a huge amount of labeled data to outperform convolutional neural networks.
  • The features learned by such a network are highly transferable and can be used for similar tasks. However, labeling a dataset is generally expensive and time-consuming.
  • Self-supervised learning can alleviate this problem by learning rich features without the need for manual annotation of labeling the dataset.
  • Existing self-supervised techniques were designed to train ConvNets. However, in recent years, Vision Transformers (ViT) have surpassed ConvNets.
  • Existing self-supervised techniques can be applied to ViTs. However, ViTs process images differently than ConvNets.

PatchRot

  • ViT splits the image into a series of input patches.
  • We randomly rotate each patch by 0°, 90°, 180° or 270°.
  • In addition to rotating patches, we create 4 more versions of the image by rotating image by 0°, 90°, 180° or 270°.
  • For every input we use 5 versions of image as input. (Note: we do not rotate image and patches together).
  • We introduce new classification heads to the ViT.
  • We use the original head for classifying the image and the new heads are used to classify the rotation angle of the patches.
  • To ensure network does not use shortcuts to classify the angles, we use a buffer B for each patch i.e. extract patch P from a P+B region.
  • Buffer reduces the image size. The self-supervised training is performed at smaller size and then adjusted to the original size by linear interpolation while adapting to downstream task.

Results

Classification accuracies for (a) CIFAR-10, (b) CIFAR-100, (c) FashionMNIST and (d) Tiny-ImageNet datasets for Supervised, RotNet and PatchRot. Solid lines denote Top-1 accuracy and the dashed lines denote Top-5 accuracy on the Y-axis.

Application to Transfer Learning

Ablation Study

Pre-training Epochs vs Final Accuracy

Attention Maps

Conclusions

  • PatchRot trains the network to predict the rotation angle of the patches as well as of the image.
  • Our approach outperforms rotation prediction and supervised learning by a fair margin showcasing the superiority of the features learned.
  • PatchRot learns to attend to the object of the image with no supervised training.

output:
A Rate-Distortion View of Uncertainty Quantification

Ifigeneia Apostolopoulou, Benjamin Eysenbach, Frank Nielsen, Artur Dubrawski

Auton Lab
Code and paper!
github.com/ifiaposto/Distance_Aware_Bottleneck

Problem: Efficient Uncertainty Quantification

Goal: reliable & lightweight models that "know what they know."

  • Principled: How much evidence?
  • Efficient: Single Model & Sampling-Free
  • Post-hoc: Works on pre-trained models

Our Method: Distance-Aware Bottleneck (DAB)

1> Treat the training dataset as a source generating training examples.

2> Map each datapoint to a distribution through a stochastic encoder.

3> The codebook contains the centroids of the encoders in terms of a statistical distance.

Uncertainty is quantified by the expected distance from the codebook.

DAB's performance on ImageNet 1-K

DAB outperforms ensembles at predicting misclassifications! ↗
DAB can better distinguish out-of-distribution images. ↗
Many fewer parameters! ↗

Key Idea: Distance-Awareness

How far is an input example from the training dataset?

Codebook Visualization

Number of CIFAR-10 test datapoints assigned to each centroid during training. Each centroid progressively attracts datapoints of the same class.

References

[1] Alemi, A. A., Fischer, I., Dillon, J. V., and Murphy, K. Deep variational information bottleneck. In International Conference on Learning Representations, 2017.
[2] Alemi, A. A., Fischer, I., and Dillon, J. V. Uncertainty in the variational information bottleneck. arXiv preprint arXiv:1807.00906, 2018.
[3] Liu, J., Lin, Z., Padhy, S., Tran, D., Bedrax Weiss, T., and Lakshminarayanan, B. Simple and principled uncertainty estimation with deterministic deep learning via distance awareness. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 2020.
[4] Banerjee, A., Merugu, S., Dhillon, I. S., Ghosh, J., and Lafferty, J. Clustering with Bregman divergences. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 2005.
[5] Van Amersfoort, J., Smith, L., Teh, Y. W., and Gal, Y. Uncertainty estimation using a single deep deterministic neural network. In International Conference on Learning Representations, 2020.

Learning to Reach Goals via Diffusion

Vineet Jain, Siamak Ravanbakhsh

McGill University
github.com/vjain/merlin

Generate trajectories that "diffuse away" from potential goals, and train a policy to reverse them

Denoising Diffusion Models

Goal-conditioned Reinforcement Learning

Goal Conditioned RL as Diffusion

Diffusion models: map any point from Gaussian → data manifold

Goal-conditioned RL: learn optimal path from any state → goal state

diffusion model
GCRL (Merlin)
target dataset
goal states
score function
policy
forward process
q(s_t | s_{t+1})
reverse process
P(s_{t+1} | s_t)

Actions that lead us away from the goal in place of Gaussian noise; Policy that reverses these actions in place of score function

A Simple 2D Navigation Problem

  • Task: Navigate to goal X; States: (x,y); Actions: unit length displacement
  • Forward process: Take random actions starting from goal
  • Reverse process: Train policy to reverse the forward trajectories

(a) Visualization of trajectories, (b) Merlin policy actions, (c) GCSL policy actions

Backward view of diffusion gives us control over the goal distribution

Effect of Time Conditioning

  • Analogous to the score function in diffusion, the Merlin policy is conditioned on time
  • This time indicates how "noisy" current state is, or how far we are from target
  • Policy takes optimal action far from goal and becomes uncertain as it approaches

The time conditioning affects policy uncertainty based on expected number of steps to reach the goal

Optimization Objective

Given an offline dataset D, we view trajectories as forward diffusion chains.

Behavior cloning maximizes the probability of reaching goal states:

θ* = arg max E [log πθ(a | s, g)]

Theorem: Given dataset D and goal distribution p(g), behavior cloning using a goal-conditioned policy maximizes a lower bound on the log-likelihood of goal states.

Constructing the Forward Process

  • Merlin: Use pre-collected offline trajectories, train policy using BC
  • Merlin-P: Learn reverse dynamics model to simulate trajectories backward
  • Merlin-NP: Use proposed trajectory stitching method

Experimental Results

Merlin outperforms previous methods, while being highly-efficient!

Conclusions and future work

  • Merlin constructs trajectories that "diffuse away" from goals and trains a policy to reverse them
  • Simple training dynamics indicate potential for scalability as shown by denoising diffusion models
  • Future work includes extending to online setting and partially observable environments

MuSc: Zero-Shot Industrial Anomaly Classification and Segmentation with Mutual Scoring of the Unlabeled Images

Xurui Li1,*, Ziming Huang1,*, Feng Xue3, Yu Zhou1,2

1Huazhong University of Science and Technology, 2Artificial Intelligence Research Institute, Wuhan JingCe Electronic Group Co.,LTD, 3University of Trento
github.com/hustvl/MuSc

Motivation

A rich amount of normal information implicit in unlabeled test images can be exploited for anomaly detection.

A discriminative characteristic: the normal image patches could find a relatively large number of similar patches in other unlabeled images, while the abnormal ones only have a few similar patches.

Contributions

  • The first method that only uses the unlabeled test images for industrial anomaly detection.
  • We reveal the potential capability of normal and abnormal patches contained in unlabeled images.
  • 21.1% PRO gains and 21.9% seg-AP gains on MVTec AD and 19.4% seg-AP gains on VisA.

Experiments

Comparison with 0-shot methods

Comparison with 4-shot methods

Framework

Mutual Scoring Mechanism

Image Ij assigns a score to each aggregated patch token of image Ii

ai,lm,r(Ij) = minn || p̂i,lm,r - p̂j,ln,r ||2

Interval Average (minimum 30%)

āi,lm,r = (1/K) Σk∈[1,K] ai,lm,r(Ik)

Mean: r and l

aim = (1/L) Σl∈{1,...,L} (1/3) Σr∈{1,3,5} āi,lm,r

RsCIN

Image-level feature Fi extracted by ViT. Similarity matrix Wi,j = Fi · Fj

Multi-window Mask Mk(i,j)

Re-Scoring D(i,i) = Σ Mk ⊙ W(i,j)

Universal module for other AD methods

LNAMD

  • Neighborhood range: 1x1, 3x3, 5x5
  • Aggregate function: Adaptive average pooling
  • Multi-degree aggregation: To segment abnormal regions of varying sizes.
1 x 1

5 x 5

[4] Roth K, Pemula L, Zepeda J, et al. Towards total recall in industrial anomaly detection[C]//CVPR 2022.

input: {"metadata": {"title": "Matrix Compression via Randomized Low Rank and Low Precision Factorization", "authors": ["Rajarshi Saha", "Varun Srivastava", "Mert Pilanci (Stanford University)"], "organizations": ["Stanford University"], "github": "github.com/pilanci-lab/LPLR", "figures": [{"fig_id": "logo_neurips", "description": "NeurIPS 会议 Logo", "figure_width": 400, "figure_height": 100}]}, "sections": [{"id": "section_1", "title": "Motivation", "content": [], "tables": [], "figures": []}, {"id": "section_2", "title": "Randomized Embeddings", "content": [], "tables": [], "figures": []}, {"id": "section_3", "title": "Our Algorithm: Low-Precision and Low Rank", "content": [], "tables": [], "figures": []}, {"id": "section_4", "title": "Data compression and Nearest Neighbors", "content": [], "tables": [], "figures": []}, {"id": "section_5", "title": "Compression of Large Language Models", "content": [], "tables": [], "figures": []}, {"id": "section_6", "title": "Approximation Error Guarantees", "content": [], "tables": [], "figures": []}, {"id": "section_7", "title": "", "content": [], "tables": [], "figures": []}]}
output:
Matrix Compression via Randomized Low Rank and Low Precision Factorization

Rajarshi Saha, Varun Srivastava, Mert Pilanci (Stanford University)

Stanford University
github.com/pilanci-lab/LPLR

Motivation

  • Matrices are ubiquitous -- can involve billions of elements making their storage and processing quite demanding.
  • Question: How to compress matrices via dimensionality reduction and quantization?
  • Several real-world matrices exhibit approximately low-rank structure due to inherent redundancy.

$A = \sum_{i=1}^{rank(A)} \sigma_i u_i v_i^T$

Leverage the approximate low-rank structure while quantizing matrices

Randomized Embeddings

$(1-\epsilon)\|z\|_2 \le \|Sz\|_2 \le (1+\epsilon)\|z\|_2 \quad \forall z \in V$

  • Subspace embeddings: Norm of vectors in the range space is approximately preserved with high probability.
  • Critical dimension: $m$ does not depend on original dimension $n$.

Random Embeddings (e.g., Gaussian) also help in quantization by reducing the dynamic range.

Our Algorithm: Low-Precision and Low Rank

Algorithm 1: LPLR: Randomized Low-Precision Low-Rank factorization
Input: Matrix A, sketch size m, Quantizers Q, Q'
1. Sample Gaussian sketching matrix S
2. Compute approximate basis by forming sketch AS
3. Quantize the approximate basis with Q to get Q(AS)
4. Find W* = arg min ||Q(AS)W - A||
5. Quantize W* using Q' to get Q'(W*)
6. Return L = Q(AS), R = Q'(W*)
  • We factorize $A \approx LR$, where entries of $L$ and $R$ are quantized with $B$ and $B'$ bits.
  • Compression ratio: $\frac{mnB + mdB'}{ndB_{nq}}$.
  • Low-precision computations are faster: Computing $Ax$ versus $L(Rx)$.

Data compression and Nearest Neighbors

DSVD: Computes best rank-k approximation and quantize factors independently.

Compression of Large Language Models

Compressing the weight matrices of Llama. We consistently observe better Frobenius norm error, except for specific layers.

Approximation Error Guarantees

R. Saha, V. Srivastava, M. Pilanci, "Matrix Compression via
Randomized Low Rank and Low Precision Factorization", 37th
Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS), 2023.
Github

output:
RNA-Protein Interaction Classification via Sequence Embeddings

Dominika Matus, Frederic Runge, Jörg K.H. Franke, Lars Gerne, Michael Uhl, Frank Hutter, Rolf Backofen

University of Freiburg, Germany; Konrad Zuse School of Excellence; ELLIS Institute Tübingen
github.com/ml-informatik-uni-freiburg.de/RNAInterAct

In a nutshell

  • RNA and proteins form complexes through binding interactions, crucial for advances in biology or medicine, such as drug development.
  • Predicting RNA-protein interactions (RPIs) computationally is challenging due to data scarcity and its bias.
  • We present RNAInterAct, the largest publicly available dataset with biologically plausible negative interactions.
  • The dataset includes sequence information and precomputed embeddings that capture meaningful information about interactors' structure and function.
  • A meticulous train/test split based on the RNA family enables evaluation on truly unseen interactions.
  • We introduce RPIembeddor, a transformer-based model for binary interaction classification and demonstrate its strong performance.

RPIembeddor

  • Protein embeddings: ESM-2
  • RNA embeddings: RNA-FM

Ablation studies

Both embeddings contribute to RPIembeddor's performance.

[1] Juanjuan Kang et al. "RNAInter v4.0: RNA interactome repository...". Nucleic Acids Research, 2022.
[2] Dharm Skandh Jain et al. "A data-driven model for predicting RNA-protein interactions...". Scientific Reports, 2018.
[3] Z. Lin et al. "Evolutionary-scale prediction of atomic-level protein structure...". Science, 2023.
[4] Jiayang Chen et al. "Interpretable RNA foundation model...". arXiv preprint, 2022.

RNAInterAct

Compilation of the RNAInterAct dataset in five steps:

  • 1. Gathering positive RPIs from the RNAInter database.
  • 2. Cross-linking large scale databases to obtain sequence information.
  • 3. Filtering.
  • 4. Annotating proteins with clan and RNA with RNA family information.
  • 5. Generating biologically relevant negative interactions.

The final dataset contains 122,217 RPIs with a 1:2 positive to negative ratio.

RNA family-based train/test split

  • Dataset split into TRinter (training) and TSfam (test) sets based on RNA families.
  • Non-homologous data guarantees evaluations on truly unseen interactions.

Evaluation on TSfam and RPI2825

*XRPI has been trained on the RPI2825 dataset.

  • RPIembeddor generalizes across data distributions.
  • Competitors behave like random classifiers.
Paper
https://openreview.net/forum?id=04MgOkuPTo
Dataset
https://ml.informatik.uni-freiburg.de/RNAInterAct

Parallel Sampling of Diffusion Models

Andy Shih, Suneel Belkhale, Stefano Ermon, Dorsa Sadigh, Nima Anari

andyshih@cs.stanford.edu

Reverse stochastic process [Song 2021]

dxt = [f(t)xt - g2(t)∇x log qt(xt)]dt + g(t)dw̄t

can reverse the SDE [Anderson 1982]

dxt/dt = f(t)xt - 1/2 g2(t)∇x log qt(xt)

can also write as ODE [Maoutsa 2020]

Picard Iterations

Solve discretized ODE by iterating until convergence

Practical Issues

out of memory? Batching: process k timesteps at a time with sliding window

approximate? No measurable degradation on standard benchmarks

We can bound total variation distance based on iteration error.

3x speedup!

Skolkovo Institute of Science and Technology

Light Schrödinger Bridge

Alexander Korotin, Nikita Gushchin, Evgeny Burnaev

(* - equal contribution)

AIRI Institute
github.com/light-sb

Motivation: Unpaired Domain Translation

The (informal) task: given samples X, Y from two domains, construct a map T which can translate new samples from the input domain to the target domain.

Examples of image unpaired domain translation

Single-cell data domain translation

Single-cell (SC) sequencing extracts features for individual cells but destroys them. Therefore, to study individual cell dynamics one needs a method to map cells between different observation times.

Background: Diffusion Schrödinger Bridge (SB)

Given two probability distributions p0, p1, how to transform p0 to p1 via a diffusion process and preserve the input-output similarity?

1. Formulation of the Schrödinger Bridge Problem: For two continuous distributions p0 and p1 on RD, is the Schrödinger bridge problem:

inf KL(P || Wε)

2. Structure of the Schrödinger Bridge: The Schrödinger bridge problem can be fully characterized by the initial distribution p0 and the Schrödinger potential φ*(x).

3. Integration of the Schrödinger Bridge SDE: The Schrödinger bridge SDE admits a closed-form solution expressed through the adjusted Schrödinger potential v*.

The minimizer T* is called the Schrödinger Bridge.

Proposed Algorithm: Light Schrödinger Bridge (LightSB)

Our solver is based on:

  • 1. Optimal parameterization of the Schrödinger bridge using mixtures of Gaussians.
  • 2. New loss function for training the Schrödinger bridge.

Algorithm 1: Light Schrödinger Bridge (LightSB)

repeat
  Sample batches {x0}, {x1}
  Update θ by using gradient descent on loss L(θ)
until converged;

Advantages:
Fast training (< 1 minute on 4 CPU cores)

Toy examples

Qualitative results of our solver applied to 2D model distributions ("Gaussian" to "swiss-roll").

Schrödinger Bridge Benchmark

Single cell experiment

Unpaired Image-to-image Translation

Qualitative results of our solver for solving the domain translation problem (in the latent space of the ALAE autoencoder).

Images resolution is 1024x1024.

(a) Male → Female

(b) Female → Male

(c) Adult → Child

(d) Child → Adult

output:
Color Equivariant Convolutional Networks

Attila Lengyel, Ombretta Strafforello, Robert-Jan Bruintjes, Alexander Gielisse, Jan van Gemert

Delft University of Technology
github.com/alengyel/CECNN

Color is a crucial visual cue for object recognition, but CNNs fail to generalize if there is data imbalance between color variations. Color invariance addresses this issue by removing all color information. We introduce Color Equivariant Convolutions (CEConvs): a novel deep learning building block that shares shape features across the color spectrum while retaining important color information.

Preliminaries

A CNN is equivariant to a transformation T if transforming the input x by T results in an equally transformed feature map:

f(T(x)) = T'(f(x))

Equivariance allows parameter sharing, resulting in better data efficiency and OOD generalization.

Transformation information is stored in extra FM dimension - invariance achieved by avg pooling.

Contributions

  • We show that CNNs benefit from color information and at the same time are not robust to color shifts.
  • We introduce Color Equivariant Convolutions (CEConvs), which allows feature sharing between colors.
  • We demonstrate that CEConvs improve robustness to train-test color shifts in the input.

Color Equivariant Convolutions

  • CEConvs implement equivariance to hue shifts.
  • Hue shifts are modeled as 3D rotations around [1,1,1] diagonal, parameterized as a 3x3 rotation matrix H.
  • CEConv input layer is defined as:

[f * ψ^l](x, k) = Σ Σ f_c(y) · H_n(k)ψ^l_c(y - x)

  • Hidden layers: cyclic permutations in color dimension.
  • Hybrid CECNNs use CEConvs in part of network.

When is color equivariance useful?

Synthetic toy settings:

  • 1. Color imbalance: simulated by long-tailed ColorMNIST, a 30-class classification problem where digits need to be classified by number (0-9) and color (red, green, blue).
  • 2. Color variations: simulated by biased ColorMNIST, a 10-class classification problem where each class c has its own characteristic hue θc defined in degrees, distributed uniformly on the hue circle.

Robustness to color shifts

Conclusions

  • Color Equivariant convolutions enable feature sharing across colors and, unlike invariance, can still use color information.
  • CECNNs outperform regular CNNs in a color imbalanced or biased setting.
  • CEConvs are computationally more expensive than regular convolutions. Hybrid CECNNs provide early equivariance with only limited compute increase.
  • CEConvs are only approximately equivariant due to clipping errors.
  • Future work: combine color and geometric transformations in equivariant convolutions.
• Group equivariant convolutional networks - Taco S. Cohen and Max Welling, in the 33rd International Conference on Machine Learning, 2016
• Color encoding in biologically-inspired convolutional neural networks - Ivet Rafegas and Maria Vanrell, in Vision Research, 2018.

output:
Generation Probabilities are Not Enough: Improving Error Highlighting in AI Code Suggestions

Helena Vasconcelos, Gagan Bansal, Adam Fourney, Vera Liao, Jennifer Wortman Vaughan

Microsoft Research

Programmers increasingly use AI generated code (e.g., from CoPilot) in IDEs!

However, generated code may be erroneous! We need to help programmers double-check AI code and identify when and where it may be incorrect.

We explore two options to highlight uncertain, potentially erroneous regions:

  • Use generation probabilities or "confidence scores" to show where the model is uncertain
  • Learn an edit model to show where programmers are most likely to make edits

Mixed-methods, within-subjects study with 30 programmers showed that edit model leads to:

  • Significantly faster task completion time
  • Significantly more localized edits
  • Stronger preference

compared to the generation probability and no-highlights

output:
[Re] Graph Edit Networks

Vid Stropnik, Maruša Oražem

University of Ljubljana, Faculty of Computer and Information Science
Data Science @ UL-FRI
Neural Information Processing Systems

Reproduced: Paaßen et. al. (ICLR, 2021)

The reproduced paper [bpa21] proposes a novel output layer for graph neural networks (GNNs), the graph edit network called (GEN). This layer yields a sequence of graph edits: node insertions, node deletions, node replacements, edge insertions and edge deletions. These finite sequences of edits, also referred to as edit scripts are general enough to describe any graph-to-graph transformation and are not only very interpretable for humans, but also computationally efficient.

The authors empirically underpin their theoretical claims about GENs by showing that they perform well in a series of graph time-series prediction tests. They define several data generating processes (DGPs), from which the GEN attempts to learn the user-defined mapping functions. Our work reproduces these experiments and evaluates the suitability of the DGPs for the conclusions made in the original paper.

DGPs & Tests

The paper evaluated several key claims on synthetic datasets, generated with data generating rules, interpreted in the following figures.

[Re] Original experimental claims

[bpa21] makes three experimental conclusions based on a series of experiments. ✓ symbols below denote a successful reproduction of results.

  • ✓ GENs outperform baselines (Variational Graph Autoencoders [KW16] & Variational Graph Recurrent nets [HHN19]) on time-series prediction tasks.
  • ✓ GENs achieve 100% accuracy on time-series prediction tasks on graphs, generated from Boolean Formulae & Peano Addition DGPs.
  • ? The runtime of forward passes of a GEN scales sub-quadratically as the number of nodes in a graph increases. The runtime of training back-passes scales approximately linearly.

While the focus here is speed, we have to note that the experiment in the original paper fails to optimize the criterion loss, thus casting doubt on the results.

Improving the experimental setup

The facts that graph-to-graph transitions are deterministic and that the space of unique graphs that may be generated by the DGPs is limited, arose some concern about the experimental setup.

We reproduced the claims (1) and (2) with the following adjustments:

  • Increased cardinality of test time series (5 -> 100)
  • Ensured no duplicates between train & test set
  • Used established random graph generation methods (ie. Erdős–Rényi)

We noticed that most sampled graphs in the tree dynamic graph experiments were un-simplifyable, so constraints were relaxed here.

Results were generally worse, but still within a reasonable margin of error.

Concluding reproduction notes

Our experimental results conclusively show that most of the claims in the original work hold. However, our work beyond the original paper emphasizes the need to pay attention to the experimental setup, especially when working with synthetically generated data, sampled from functions, rather than gathered from an external system.

References

[bpa21] Paaßen, Benjamin, et al. "Graph edit networks." In: International Conference on Learning Representations. 2020

[Gar70] Martin Gardner. "The fantastic combinations of John Conway's new solitaire game of life." In: Sc. Am., 223:20-123, 1970

[KW16] Thomas N. Kipf and Max Welling. "Variational graph auto-encoders" In: Proceedings of the NIPS 2016 Workshop on Bayesian Deep Learning, 2016

[HHN19] Hajiramezanali, Ehsan, et. al. "Variational graph recurrent neural networks." In: Proceedings of the 32nd International Conference on Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2019), pp. 10700-10710, 2019

[LKF05] Leskovec, Jure, Jon Kleinberg, and Christos Faloutsos. "Graphs over time: densification laws, shrinking diameters and possible explanations." In: Proceedings of the eleventh ACM SIGKDD international conference on Knowledge discovery in data mining. 2005.

Visual Correspondence Hallucination

Hugo Germain¹, Vincent Lepetit¹, Guillaume Bourmaud²

1. Low-overlap keypoint matching

  • Keypoint matching is key for fundamental matrix & camera pose estimation
  • In low-overlap scenarios however, finding correspondences is very challenging

SuperPoint + SuperGlue

Correct matches within image boundaries provide poor constraints

2. Introducing NeurHal

We predict correspondence maps on extended field-of-views

Training is done on both covisible and non-covisible samples

We resort to a Transformer-based image processing backbone

3. Application to absolute camera pose estimation

We provide quantitative analysis of NeurHal's ability to hallucinate

NeurHal on validation image pair (novel scene)

NeurHal achieves sota on absolute pose estimation for low-overlap image pairs

More results in our paper !

Target
Predicted NRE Map

CABINET: Content Relevance Based Noise Reduction for Table Question Answering

Sohan Patnaik, Heril Changwal, Milan Aggarwal, Sumit Bhatia, Yaman Kumar, Balaji Krishnamurthy

Media and Data Science Research Lab, Adobe, India
github.com/adobe-research/CABINET
Contact: milaggar@adobe.com

LLMs have been used for Table Understanding

Large Tabular Data degrades LLM Performance

Hard Decomposition can cause Information Loss

Our Approach: CABINET assigns Relevance Score to Tabular Data Tokens through Two Modules - 1) Unsupervised Relevance Scorer and 2) Weakly Supervised Relevant Cell Highlighter

CABINET (despite having less #params) outperforms several SoTA Methods

CABINET is more Robust to Noise and Table Perturbations

RA – Row Addition

RP – Row Permutation

CP – Column Permutation

CR – Cell Replacement

CABINET is more Performant while Handling Irrelevant Data in Large Tables

Clustering URS Output Representations is Helpful

Ablation Study

Using Both Modules gives Best Performance

output:
DARTFormer: Finding The Best Type Of Attention

Jason Ross Brown, Yiren Zhao, Ilia Shumailov, Robert D. Mullins

University of Cambridge, Imperial College London, University of Oxford
github.com/jasonrossbrown/dartformer
jrb239@cam.ac.uk, a.zhao@imperial.ac.uk, ilia.shumailov@chch.ox.ac.uk, robert.mullins@cl.cam.ac.uk

Motivation

  • There is a wide range of efficient Transformer attention mechanisms whose relative performance often depends on the task
  • Different attention heads are supposed to pick up different patterns
  • DARTS-like neural architecture search is highly effective at finding good network topologies

DARTFormer

Attention Search

  • Idea: Can we apply a DARTS-like method to identify good and bad attention mechanisms?
  • By training a supernetwork with heads that have different attention types in each layer, we can then use masked validation to identify the current best and worse attention type for that layer.

Optimal Homogeneous Attention

Applying the attention search idea to a single layer Transformer and picking the best performing attention, we can hope to identify the best attention for that task when operating in a full Transformer model.

Optimal Heterogenous Attention

  • Method 1: Attention search on a single layer pruning the worst heads until we have the right size for a single layer. Stack this layer and retrain from scratch.
  • Method 2: Attention search on a single layer and then take the top 4 scoring mechanisms. Stack a uniform mix and retrain from scratch.
  • Method 3: Attention search across many layers, picking the best attention in each one. Retrain from scratch with possible different attention in each layer

Experimental Results

  • Homogeneous search works on 2/3 tasks
  • Heterogeneous attention fails to outperform the best homogeneous models

Conclusion

  • A DARTS-like method can often be used to find the best attention for a task by training only a single layer
  • Searching for and using heterogeneous 'mixed attention' Transformers does not give good results.
  • Perhaps different mechanisms do not provide as large functional biases, or the biases are less useful for learning good representations in the attention layer than was previously thought

output:
Harvard John A. Paulson
School of Engineering and Applied Sciences

Bayesian Inverse Transition Learning for Offline Settings

Leo Benac, Sonali Parbhoo, Finale Doshi-Velez

Harvard University
github.com/dtak/bitl

Summary

  • Learning a model is hard under low-coverage data
  • Having a model help be more data efficient
  • Inverse Transition Learning infers the dynamics using near-optimal demonstrations
  • We create a set of constraints to estimate in a gradient-free way an empirical posterior distribution over the transition dynamics T, where each sample has near-optimal guarantees
  • Show that planned policies are highly performant and exhibit lower variance across different batches of datas compared to MLE estimates
  • Explain how quantifying the uncertainty of the dynamics help planning more informative policies than the expert policy and the one obtained from MLE estimates

What is Inverse Transition Learning?

Inverse Transition Learning

Infer the dynamics T given near-optimal demonstrations D, the reward function R and a near-optimal expert policy πe, where πe is assumed to only take actions that are ε-close to the best unknown action a*.

Why is it hard?

  • Number of parameters grows with the dimensions of state and action spaces
  • Trade-off between a model that is expressive enough and one that is small enough to learn
  • Lack of coverage in offline inverse settings

Why do we care?

  • Data efficiency
  • Simulation of new data
  • Off-Policy Evaluation
  • Counterfactual Reasoning

Baselines

  • TMLE Maximum Likelihood Estimate obtained from the expert demonstrations D
  • P(T(s',a)|D) = Dir(Ns,a + 1|s,a) Probabilistic model over T without making any optimality assumption. Where 1 is a vector of 1's of dimension |S| and Ns,a is the number of transitions from state s and action a in the batch data D

Constraints

Using the reward R and near-optimal expert policy πe we create a set of constraints with respect to the dynamics T to impose the values of the actions of the expert to be superior to the ones of unseen actions.

Method

  • Sample from P(T|D) and only keep samples that satisfies the constraints.
  • Create empirical posterior P(T|D, πe) with accepted samples. This will be the estimate of the posterior on T given that the demonstrations from the expert are near-optimal

Metric

Q*metric(π̂) = ∑s∈S ( Q*(s, a*) - ∑a∈A π̂(a|s)Q*(s, a) )

Results

• MLE

T̂ = TMLE —Value Iteration→ (πMLE, QMLE) = (π̂, Q̂) —Compute Results→

• P(T|D, πe) and P(T|D)

{T̂(i)}i=11000 ~ Posterior —Value Iteration→ {(π̂(i), Q̂(i))}i=11000
—Empirical Mean→ {(π̂, Q̂)} —Compute Results→

  • P(T|D, πe) outperforms baselines by being more accurate and avoiding low value actions
  • P(T|D, πe) exhibits significantly less variance over different datasets
  • P(T|D, πe) allows quantifying uncertainty on corresponding policies

Acknowledgements

This material is based upon work supported by the National Science Foundation under Grant No. IIS-2007076. Any opinions, findings, and conclusions or recommendations expressed in this material are those of the author(s) and do not necessarily reflect the views of the National Science Foundation.

On the convergence of mSGD and AdaGrad for stochastic optimization

Ruinan Jin, Yu Xing, Xingkang He

UCAS
KTH
University of Notre Dame
github.com/optimization-research

Algorithms:

Contributions:

  • We prove that the iterates of mSGD are asymptotically convergent to a connected set of stationary points for possibly non-convex loss function almost surely.
  • We quantify the convergence rate of mSGD for the loss functions. Through this convergence rate we can get a theoretical explanation of why mSGD can be seen as an acceleration of SGD.
  • We prove the iterates of AdaGrad are asymptotically convergent to a connected set of stationary points almost surely for possible non-convex loss functions.

Main assumptions:

Assumption 1 Noise sequence {ξn} are mutually independent and independent of θ1 and v0, such that g(x) = Eξn(g(x, ξn)) for any x ∈ ℝN.

Assumption 2 (Loss function assumption) Loss function g(θ) satisfies the following conditions: 1) g(θ) is non-negative and continuously differentiable. 2) The set of stationary points is not empty. 3) ∇g(θ) satisfies Lipschitz condition. 4) There is a scalar M > 0 for any θ and positive integer n.

Assumption 3 Momentum coefficient α ∈ (0,1) and the sequence of step size εn is positive, monotonically decreasing to zero.

Assumption 4 (Loss function assumption) Loss function g(θ) satisfies the following conditions: 1) g(θ) is non-negative and continuously differentiable. 2) For any i ∈ {1,2,...,n}, it holds that...

Assumption 5 Loss function g(θ) in equation 5 satisfies the following conditions: 1) g(θ) is non-negative and continuously differentiable. 2) The gradient ∇g(θ) satisfies the Lipschitz condition. 3) There are two constants M' > 0 and a > 0.

Main results:

Theorem 1 Consider the mSGD in equation 3. If Assumptions 1-3 hold, then for ∀θ1 ∈ ℝN and ∀v0 ∈ ℝN, there exists a connected set J* ⊆ J such that the iterate θn is convergent to the set J* almost surely.

Theorem 2 Consider the mSGD in equation 3 with the noise following a uniform sampling distribution. If Assumptions 1-4 hold, for ∀v0 ∈ ℝN and ∀θ1 ∈ ℝN, it holds that:

Theorem 3 Consider the AdaGrad in equation 5. If Assumptions 1 and 5 hold, then for ∀θ1 ∈ ℝN and S0 = 0, there exists a connected component of the set J* ⊆ J, such that the estimate θn is convergent to the set J* almost surely.

Proof outline for Theorems 1-2:

The proof is in light of the Lyapunov method. We aim to prove that g(θn) is convergent a.s., and then to prove ∇g(θn) → 0 a.s. With these two results, we are able to get θn → J* a.s.

Step 1: We prove mSGD is a stable algorithm, i.e., E(g(θn)) < +∞, ∀n, in Lemma 1. The idea is to prove that a weighted sum of the loss function value, i.e.,

is bounded through a recursion formula (a rough form).

Step 2: From Lemma 1 and Lemma 8, we are able to prove that ΣA(n)||vn||² < +∞, we are able to prove that Σ||vn||² and Σ||∇g(θn)||² are convergent a.s. respectively, as stated in Lemma 7 and Lemma 8.

Step 3: We divide g(θn) into three terms:

Step 4: By Lemma 9 and the convergence of g(θn) in martingale-difference sum (Lemma 5), we prove that ΣCn(∇g(θn, ξn) - ∇g(θn)) is convergent a.s. Then we prove g(θn) is convergent a.s.

Step 5: After the proof of the convergence of mSGD, we analyze the iterates of Fn. Then under a new assumption lim inf ||∇g(θ)||²/ (g(θ) - gi) ≥ s > 0, we are able to obtain the convergent rate of mSGD.

Flexible Attention-Based Multi-Policy Fusion for Efficient Deep Reinforcement Learning

Zih-Yun Chiu, Yi-Lin Tuan, William Yang Wang, Michael C. Yip

University of California San Diego
University of California Santa Barbara
github.com/zihyun-chiu/KIAN

Why Knowledge-Grounded Reinforcement Learning?

  • What learning capabilities do humans possess, yet RL agents still missing? [1,2]
  • Knowledge-Acquirable: Develop unique policies by observing.
  • Compositional: Combine policies from multiple sources to form a knowledge set.
  • Incremental: Arbitrarily remove outdated policies or add new ones.
  • Sample-Efficient: Require fewer interactions.
  • Generalizable: Be able to apply previously learned policies to unseen tasks.
  • KGRL: An RL paradigm aiming to learn with external policies and achieve the five properties above.

Method: Knowledge-Inclusive Attention Network (KIAN)

KIAN: An actor that separates knowledge representations from their selection mechanism.

Naive policy fusion leads to challenges:

  • Discrete vs Continuous action space
  • Entropy Imbalance in maximum entropy KGRL

Discrete Action Policy

π(·|s_t) = w_in π_in(·|s_t) + Σ w_gj softmax(β_t,gj π_gj(·|s_t))

Continuous Action Prediction

π_in(·|s_t) = N(μ_t,in, σ^2_t,in), π_gj(·|s_t) = N(μ_t,gj, σ^2_t,gj)

How well are the KGRL properties satisfied?

Environments:

  • Discrete decision making (MiniGrid)
  • Continuous control (OpenAI-Robotics)

Main Results: Sample Efficiency & Compositional

Generalizability

How entropy imbalance affects?

Addressing entropy imbalance is important!

[1] A. Bandura. Social Learning Theory. Prentice-Hall, 1977.
[2] Leslie Pack Kaelbling. The foundation of efficient robot learning. Science, 2020.
[3] Peng Zhang et al. KoGuN: Accelerating deep reinforcement learning via integrating human suboptimal knowledge. IJCAI, 2020.
[4] Janarthanan Rajendran et al. Attend, adapt and transfer: Attentive deep architecture for adaptive transfer from multiple sources. ICLR, 2017.

Cresswell"], "organizations": [], "github": "", "figures": [{"fig_id": "layer6_logo", "description": "Layer 6 AI 公司 Logo", "figure_width": 180, "figure_height": 60}]}, "sections": [{"id": "section_1", "title": "Background", "content": [], "tables": [], "figures": []}, {"id": "section_2", "title": "Our Geometric Framework", "content": [], "tables": [], "figures": []}, {"id": "section_3", "title": "Explaining Memorization", "content": [], "tables": [], "figures": []}, {"id": "section_4", "title": "Empirical Verification", "content": [], "tables": [], "figures": []}, {"id": "section_5", "title": "", "content": ["[1] Rombach, Blattmann, Lorenz, Esser, Ommer. \"High-resolution image synthesis with latent diffusion models\". CVPR 2022.[2] Webster. A reproducible extraction of training images from diffusion models. arXiv 2305.08694.[3] Kamkari, Ross, Hosseinzadeh, Cresswell, Loaiza-Ganem. \"A geometric view of data complexity: Efficient local intrinsic dimension estimation with diffusion models\". arXiv 2406.03537.[4] Somepalli, Singla, Goldblum, Geiping, Goldstein \"Understanding and mitigating copying in diffusion models\". NeurIPS 2023.[5] Wen, Liu, Chen, Lyu \"Detecting, explaining, and mitigating memorization in diffusion models\". ICML 2023."], "tables": [], "figures": []}]}
output:
A Geometric Framework for Understanding Memorization in Generative Models

Brendan Leigh Ross, Hamidreza Kamkari, Zhaoyan Liu, Tongzi Wu, George Stein, Gabriel Loaiza-Ganem, Jesse C. Cresswell

Layer 6 AI, Toronto, Canada

Background

Memorization and Overfitting in Deep Generative Models

  • Deep generative models are known to memorize and reproduce their training data
  • This behaviour reduces model utility and may have severe privacy and legal implications
  • Contributing factors [4]: data complexity, duplication of training points, and highly specific conditioning
  • There is as yet no systematic framework describing memorization in DGMs

The Manifold Hypothesis

  • High-dimensional data lies on a manifold M* with multiple components of varying dimensionalities
  • DGMs also generate samples from a manifold Mθ with the goal of matching Mθ to M*
  • The local intrinsic dimension of x ∈ M* (resp. x ∈ Mθ), denoted LID*(x) (resp. LIDθ(x)), is the dimension of the manifold component containing x
  • Intuitively, the LID of x captures the complexity of the datapoint x and constraints on the dataset's structure

Our Geometric Framework

(a) Exact memorization (LIDθ(x) = 0)
(b) Some Memorization (LIDθ(x) = 1)
(c) No memorization (LIDθ(x) = 2)

Figure 1: LID values for three red model manifolds with different degrees of memorization of the three leftmost datapoints, which are depicted as dark blue crosses. The true manifold is light blue.

We propose that x ∈ M* is memorized when LIDθ(x) is small.

  • This encompasses both exact copying of training images (LIDθ(x) = 0) and copying of styles or parts of an image as in Figure 2 (LIDθ(x) > 0 but still small)

From this definition, memorization can be split into two types:

1. LIDθ(x) < LID*(x): overfitting-driven memorization (OD-Mem), in which the model fails to generalize to the ground truth manifold correctly (Figure 1)

2. LIDθ(x) = LID*(x): data-driven memorization (DD-Mem), in which the ground truth data distribution has been learned correctly, but itself contains very few degrees of freedom at x

Figure 2: 8 images along a relatively low-dimensional manifold learned by Stable Diffusion v1.5. The first is a real image from LAION, and the remainder were generated by the model.

Explaining Memorization

The geometric framework explains memorization-related phenomena in the literature:

Duplicated Data It has been observed that memorization occurs when training points are duplicated. Here we identify that data duplication is equivalent to an LID of 0.

Theorem (Informal). With high probability and a sufficiently large dataset, duplicate samples occur at x0 if and only if LID*(x0) = 0.

Complexity. [4] observes that simple images are memorized more often. This is neatly explained by the fact that they have lower LID*.

Conditioning. Highly-specific conditioning inputs encourage the reproduction of memorized samples. We explain this by the fact that conditioning on a prompt c decreases LID.

Proposition. LIDθ(x|c) ≤ LIDθ(x).

Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG). A large classifier-free guidance vector in diffusion models correlates strongly with memorization [5]. This is explainable by observations in the literature that faster explosion of model norms during sampling corresponds to lower LIDθ.

Empirical Verification

We can test this framework using the LIDθ estimator of [3]:

von Mises Distribution We train a diffusion model on a von Mises distribution; in Figure 3b we depict model samples with their corresponding LIDs, and a couple of samples are clearly memorized with LIDθ(x) = 0, while the remaining samples have LIDθ(x) = 1.

(a) Ground truth manifold.
(b) Model samples.

Figure 3: Training a diffusion model on a von Mises distribution.

Stable Diffusion on LAION, COCO, and Tuxemon We compute LIDθ of Stable Diffusion [1] on:

  • Unmemorized images from a mix of LAION Aesthetics 6.5+, COCO, and Tuxemon
  • Memorized images from LAION [2]

We find that low LIDs correspond better to memorized images than PNG size, a proxy for image complexity.

(a) Unconditional LID
(b) Conditional LID
(c) PNG compression

Figure 4: Density histograms for each memorization metric on the different datasets.

[1] Rombach, Blattmann, Lorenz, Esser, Ommer. "High-resolution image synthesis with latent diffusion models". CVPR 2022.
[2] Webster. A reproducible extraction of training images from diffusion models. arXiv 2305.08694.
[3] Kamkari, Ross, Hosseinzadeh, Cresswell, Loaiza-Ganem. "A geometric view of data complexity: Efficient local intrinsic dimension estimation with diffusion models". arXiv 2406.03537.
[4] Somepalli, Singla, Goldblum, Geiping, Goldstein "Understanding and mitigating copying in diffusion models". NeurIPS 2023.
[5] Wen, Liu, Chen, Lyu "Detecting, explaining, and mitigating memorization in diffusion models". ICML 2023.

output:
Uni[MASK]: Unified Inference In Sequential Decision Problems

Micah Carroll, Orr Paradise, Jessy Lin, Raluca Georgescu, Mingfei Sun, David Bignell, Stephanie Milani, Katja Hofmann, Matthew Hausknecht, Anca Dragan, Sam Devlin

UC Berkeley, Microsoft Research, Carnegie Mellon University
github.com/micahcarroll/unimask
Full paper here
Accepted as Oral

Background: BERT and Randomized Masking

In NLP, having the model predict masked input words has been a powerful idea for learning general-purpose representations of language.

Can the same idea be useful in sequential decision problems?

Tokens are states, actions, and rewards. Training for predicting masked inputs amounts to training on different inference tasks (e.g. BC, future prediction, etc.).

Our question: by training on random mask prediction tasks, can we train a single multi-task model for any task in a sequential decision problem?

Inference Tasks as Masking Schemes

Key idea: any inference in sequential decision problems can be expressed as a sequence masking. The model receives a subset of observed tokens and has to predict some other subset.

Behavioral Cloning
Reward-conditioned
Goal-conditioned
Waypoint-conditioned
Past inference
Future inference
Forward Dynamics
Inverse Dynamics

Many possible training regimes...

Train specialized multi-task, or any-task models with a single, unified architecture

1. Can one train on all possible tasks?

Training a single model on all tasks (via random masking) not only works, but can do as well or better than specialized models!

2. Which training regime is best for one task?

While pre-training on multiple maskings does well, fine-tuning this general model one can improve performance further.

3. Which training regime is best for n tasks?

Training on all possible tasks (with random masking) can sometimes enable to do better than training on the n tasks of interest.

Results: a single set of model parameters for all tasks

Discrete Minigrid Environment

random masking pre-training + finetuning
Specialized models

A single Uni[MASK] model can be used for arbitrary inference tasks out-of-the-box when trained on random masking.

Continuous Control (Mujoco Maze2D)

Pre-training and fine-tuning on single-tasks achieves the best performance among Uni[MASK] regimes.

Limitations and Future Work

  • Scale up to more complex environments
  • Systematically investigate how multi-task performance varies with larger context windows
  • Train models to roll out trajectories conditioned on other useful properties (e.g., style-matching).

Conclusions:

The Uni[MASK] framework enables models to:

  • 1. Naturally represent any inference task
  • 2. Easily perform multi-task training with no architecture changes
  • 3. Train single models to do all tasks with random-mask pre-training, which perform similarly to specialized models and often surpass them after fine-tuning.

output:
Fast, Scalable, Warm-Start Semidefinite Programming with Spectral Bundling and Sketching

Rico Angell, Andrew McCallum

University of Massachusetts Amherst
github.com/iesl/usbs

Motivation

  • Semidefinite programming (SDP) is an important tool for many applications
  • Many existing methods for solving or approximately solving SDPs
  • Need for a method that is fast, scalable, and provably correct for massive SDPs
  • Need a method where hyperparameters can modulate memory, accuracy, speed, and scalability of solving general SDPs

Problem Setup

max <C, X>
s.t. A_i X = b_i
A_i X ≤ b_i
X ≥ 0

  • Want to solve general SDPs with both equality and inequality constraints
  • Want to have theoretical guarantees about convergence to optimal solution
  • Assume cost matrix C is sparse and a upper bound α ≥ tr(X*) exists
  • Assume the number of constraints is small compared to the problem size
  • Want to be able to solve SDPs where X will not fit in memory
  • Want solver to be able to take advantage of a warm-start initialization

Unified Spectral Bundling

Reformulated SDP as a the following penalized dual objective:

f(y) := α [λ_max(C - A*y)]_+ + <b, y> + ι_y(y)

  • Solve the SDP by minimizing f using a proximal bundle method
  • The size of V determines the rank k of each update, specified by the user
  • Only update y_t+1 if progress is sufficient
  • Always use y_t+1 to create X_t+1, V_t+1 is the top-k eigenvectors of C - A*y_t+1
  • Analytical solution to the proximal bundle method lead to the following updates

Scaling with Matrix Sketching

  • Only need to keep track of projections of X such as <C, X> and AX
  • All of the memoization can be done efficiently due to linearity of operations
  • Apply low-rank matrix sketching techniques to enable scaling to problems where X cannot fit in memory — track projection P instead of X

Results

Max-Cut

Traveling Salesman Problem

Clustering with ∃-constraints

UMassAmherst

Manning College of Information & Computer Sciences

"organizations": ["The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)"], "github": "github.com/GeoReasoner", "figures": [{"fig_id": "logo_icml", "description": "ICML 会议 Logo", GeoReasoner: Geo-localization with Reasoning in Street Views using a Large Vision-Language Model

Ling Li, Yu Ye, Bingchuan Jiang, Wei Zeng

The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)
github.com/GeoReasoner

Introduction

This work tackles the problem of geo-localization with a new paradigm using a large vision-language model (LVLM) augmented with human inference knowledge.

Challenge: The scarcity of data for training the LVLM - existing street-view datasets often contain numerous low-quality images lacking visual clues, and lack any reasoning inference.

Dataset: We devise a CLIP-based network to quantify the locatability of street-view images, resulting in the creation of a new dataset comprising highly locatable street views.

Model: GeoReasoner, which undergoes fine-tuning through dedicated reasoning and location-tuning stages, outperforms counterpart LVLMs by more than 25% at country-level and 38% at city-level geo-localization tasks.

Data Curation

Street-View Images: A total of over 130k street-view images with geo-tags collected from Google Street View.

Textual Clues: A total of over 3K textual clues that encapsulate rich geo-localization information from GeoGuessr and Tuxun communities.

Experiments

Locatability Quantization Method
GeoReasoner Performance
Results of the ablation experiments
Qualitative Results

Geo-localization with Reasoning

The architecture of GeoReasoner consists of three modules: Vision Encoder, VL Adapter and Pre-trained LLM. The model undergoes a two-fold supervised fine-tuning process: reasoning tuning and location tuning.

Contributions

  • We present a new paradigm that leverages an LVLM and external knowledge of human inference for geolocation with reasoning from street-view images.
  • We introduce the concept of locatability and devise a CLIP-based network to quantify the degree of locatability in street-view images.
  • We propose GeoReasoner, an LVLM that outperforms existing geo-localization models and provides detailed reasoning for the inferred results.

output:
Fine-Tuning Language Models with Just Forward Passes

Sadhika Malladi*, Tianyu Gao*, Eshaan Nichani, Alex Damian, Jason D. Lee, Danqi Chen, Sanjeev Arora. Princeton University

Princeton University
github.com/princeton-nlp/MeZO
[1] J.C. Spall. Multivariate stochastic approximation... (1992).
[2] Hu et al. LoRA: Low-rank adaptation... (ICLR '22).
[3] Li & Liang. Prefix Tuning: Optimizing Continuous Prompts... (ACL '21).

Fine-tuning LLMs with backpropagation requires a prohibitively large amount of memory.
We propose MeZO, a memory-efficient zeroth-order optimizer.
Costs the same memory as inference without sacrificing much performance.
With one A100, MeZO can tune a 30B model while Adam can only tune a 2.7B model.

MeZO Algorithm

Classic zeroth-order (ZO) gradient estimate with two forward passes: SPSA [1]

∇L(θ; B) ≈ (L(θ + εz; B) - L(θ - εz; B)) / (2ε) * z

Memory-efficient ZO-SGD (MeZO): resample z so SGD with SPSA uses no extra memory besides inference cost.

(by first sampling a random seed and later restoring the random seed to make sure the sampled z is consistent)

Why does it work?

Classical results: ZO slows down by number of parameters d.

Our case: fine-tuning a pre-trained model with a prompt, so fine-tuning looks like pre-training!

Then: effective rank of Hessian r is small, convergence rates depend on r instead of d (descent lemma).

Experiments

Our examples cover medium-sized masked LMs (RoBERTa-large) and large autoregressive LMs (OPT-13B, 30B, 66B).

Both full-parameter and parameter-efficient fine-tuning (e.g., LoRA, prefix-tuning).

We experiment on classification, multiple-choice, and generation tasks.

  • 1. On RoBERTa-large, MeZO outperforms zero-shot and linear probing.
  • 2. MeZO is compatible with both full-parameter and parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT), such as LoRA [2], prefix-tuning [3].
  • 3. On OPT-13B, MeZO achieves on par performance with Adam on 7/11 tasks, while saving up to 12x memory.
  • 4. MeZO also beats memory-equivalent methods like in-context learning and linear probing.

5. MeZO can scale up to 66B models.

6. MeZO works with non-differentiable objectives.

When should you use MeZO

  • efficient, fuzzy gradient estimate (e.g., data selection)
  • optimize non-differentiable objectives (e.g., with a reward model)
  • study huge models but don't have many GPUs

When shouldn't you use MeZO

  • Does fine-tuning a model do X?
  • I have a new task without obvious prompts
  • I only want to study small models

Accurate And Scalable Estimation Of Epistemic Uncertainty For Graph Neural Networks

Puja Trivedi, Mark Heimann, Rushil Anirudh, Danai Koutra, Jayaraman J. Thiagarajan

University of Michigan
GEMS LAB
Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory
github.com/gems-lab/G-Delta-UQ

Stochastic Centering: Single Model Ensembles

NTKs for (Graph) Neural Networks are not shift invariant

The variability over shifts captures epistemic uncertainty!

  • Stochastic Centering creates a relative representation for an input, x, in terms of a random anchor, c.
  • During training, the anchor is randomized, thus implicitly sampling different hypotheses.
  • During testing, predictions are computed over multiple anchors.
  • Discrepancy across anchors choices captures epistemic uncertainty!

G Δ-UQ: Stochastic Centering for GNNs

  • Since graphs are variable sized and discrete, we introduce three anchoring strategies tailored for GNNs.
  • Induce varying levels of stochasticity, trading off hypothesis diversity and the semantic diversity of the anchoring distribution.

We propose G Δ-UQ, a novel training paradigm for improving calibration with GNNs

  • Scalable and Lightweight
  • Robust to Distribution Shifts
  • Supports Pretrained Models
  • Easy to Implement
# Pseudocode snippet for G Δ-UQ implementation

G Δ-UQ is easy to implement, with only a few changes to standard GNNs!

Improved Node Classification Calibration

We evaluate G Δ-UQ with node feature anchoring on:

  • 4 Datasets
  • 2 Distribution shifts
  • 8 Post Hoc Calibration

G Δ-UQ matches or surpasses accuracy and calibration of the vanilla GNN on 8/8 datasets.

Better Graph Classification Calibration

G Δ-UQ Improves Calibration of Pretrained GNNs

G Δ-UQ Estimates are Effective in Safety Critical Tasks

  • G Δ-UQ achieves the best OOD detection AUROC on 5/6 datasets.
  • G Δ-UQ also performs well on the generalization gap prediction tasks.

G Δ-UQ Improves Graph Classification Calibration with Different MPNNs

G Δ-UQ is flexible to message passing layer. Required level of stochasticity may vary for dataset/GNN.

Check out the G Δ-UQ Family!

Try E Δ-UQ for link prediction calibration!

SiT Dataset : Socially Interactive Pedestrian Trajectory Dataset for Social Navigation Robots

Jongwook Bae, Jungho Kim, Junyong Yun, Changwon Kang, Jeongseon Choi, Kim Chanhyeok, Junho Lee, Jun Won Choi*

SPA Laboratory, Department of Artificial Intelligence, Hanyang University, Seoul, Republic of Korea
github.com/hanyang-spa/SiT-Dataset

Contributions

  • Static collection: Many pedestrian trajectory datasets were collected from fixed positions, limiting variability.
  • Missing ego motion: Many existing robot datasets lack ego-motion information.
  • Vehicle-centric bias: Most autonomous datasets were collected from vehicles, exhibiting a gap from realistic robot-pedestrian interaction behavior.

Overview

  • Real-world context: Collected data from dense areas like campuses and public roads.
  • Comprehensive data: Sequential raw data from various sensors; 60 scenes with 60K images and 12K point cloud frames at 10 Hz.
  • Unique features: Emphasis on Human-Robot Interactions (HRI); Precise multi-sensor synchronization; Multi-layered indoor & outdoor semantic maps.

Robot Setup

  • 1 x Husky UGV platform
  • 2 x Velodyne VLP-16
  • 5 x Basler a2A1920-51gc cameras
  • 1 x MTi-680G IMU & RTK
  • 1 x VectorNav VN-100 IMU

Details of SiT Dataset

Comparison with other datasets

Examples of human-robot interactions

Tasks and Benchmark

Perception tasks

  • 3D Object Detection based on image or point clouds
  • 3D Multi-Object Tracking
  • Trajectory Prediction

Benchmark

  • Plan to release benchmarks and pre-trained models.
  • Challenges open on Eval.AI (Feb. 2024)

Samples of dataset

Hallway
Cafeteria
Outdoor Alley

output:
Near-real-time monitoring of global ocean carbon sink

Piyu Ke, Xiaofan Gui, Wei Cao, Dezhi Wang, Ce Hou, Lixing Wang, Xuanren Song, Yun Li, Biqing Zhu, Jiang Bian, Stephen Sitch, Philippe Ciais, Pierre Friedlingstein, Zhu Liu

Contact: xiaofangui@microsoft.com

Introduction

We present Carbon Monitor Ocean (CMO-NRT), a novel dataset providing near-real-time monthly gridded estimates of global surface ocean fugacity of CO₂ (fCO₂) and ocean-atmosphere CO₂ flux from January 2022 to July 2023. Leveraging Convolutional Neural Networks and semi-supervised learning, our highly accurate models enhance timely climate change mitigation efforts.

Environmental factors
Predictors
➔
Global ocean carbon sink
Target
  • Environmental factors influencing and reflecting oceanic carbon sink variations
  • Reconstructing global oceanic carbon sink using ML methods with global environmental data

Model

Result

Our models maintain accuracy with errors below a 5% threshold (NRMSE), most models have losses below 2%

Our Paper
github.com/carbonmonitor-ocean
Our Website
Tsinghua University
