Quantifying Network Similarity using Graph Cumulants

Gecia Bravo-Hermsdorff, Lee M. Gunderson, Pierre-André Maugis, Carey E. Priebe

Department of Statistical Science, University College London
Google Research Zürich
Department of Applied Mathematics and Statistics, Johns Hopkins University
github.com/gecia
TL;DR:

Graph cumulants perform better and are more intuitive than the typical subgraph statistics.

Using only moments is awkward:

"The length of a human averages 1.7 meters, and their average squared length is 2.9 square meters"

Using cumulants is easier to understand:

"The length of a human has: a variance of 0.01 meters, a standard deviation of 0.1 meters, a relative fluctuation of 6%"

Graph Cumulants: the Better Subgraph Statistics

Erdős-Rényi is the new Gaussian...

...degrees are encoded in stars...

...clustering is encoded in cycles...

...and bipartite-ness as well!

But are graph cumulants better for testing?

Apples-to-Apples: A Two-Sample Test

When estimating the covariance... the "hard" part uses a combinatorial disjoint union rule.

Combinatorial Construction of Cumulants

Graph Cumulants Clearly Conquer

Graph cumulants outperform subgraph densities in general...

...and graph cumulants also work for single graph samples!

Why do graph cumulants perform better?

...because their fluctuations look more Normal!

Graph Cumulants in the (semi-)Wild!

Journal of Machine Learning Research vol. 24 (2023) 1-27

output:
Self-supervised detection of atmospheric phenomena from remotely sensed synthetic aperture radar imagery

Yannik Glaser, Peter Sadowski, Justin Stopa

Information and Computer Sciences, University of Hawai'i at Mānoa
Department of Ocean and Resources Engineering, University of Hawaii at Manoa
github.com/yannikglaser/sar-self-supervised

Sentinel-1 ocean surface data and atmospheric stability

The European Space Agency provides unprecedented monitoring of Earth's oceans through a network of Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) satellites called Sentinel-1. Imagery from these satellites captures a variety of atmosphere and ocean surface phenomena including waves, atmospheric turbulence, ocean fronts, and marine biology.

The Sentinel-1 (S-1) satellites are equipped with a C-band synthetic aperture radar instrument to perform continuous high-resolution day-and-night imaging unaffected by cloud cover. One of the four acquisition modes of the SAR sensor, primarily for measuring ocean waves across the global open ocean, is the 'Wave' mode (WV).

MC
WS/MC
WS
OT
NV

Examples of the 20 km by 20 km WV images at 5m resolution are shown to the right.

Sentinel-1 WV image acquisition process

Mesoscale convective cells, wind streaks, and negligible atmospheric variability are atmospheric phenomena representing a spectrum of stability regimes along the oceanic surface layer, corresponding to unstable, near stable, and stable conditions respectively.

Key insights:

  • We use deep learning to classify atmospheric phenomena in a hand-labeled dataset of synthetic aperture radar WaVe images.
  • We attempt to use contrastive learning on a dataset of 3 million unlabeled images to pretrain a model before finetuning on labeled data.
  • We show that the default setup of the SwAV contrastive learning framework does not outperform standard transfer learning from ImageNet weights.

Dataset

Unlabeled dataset. This dataset consists of three years (2017, 2018, 2019) of Copernicus S-1 A and B observations and contains 2,943,550 images, of which 90% are used for training and 10% for validation.

Labeled dataset. The labeled data is multi-label, allowing for multiple positive classes per WV image, and sampled randomly across the globe. Images were labeled by at least one expert and four classes of interest are used: (1) MC, (2) WS, (3) NV, and (4) other (OT). In total, we have 2,300 samples which are stratified and split into 60% training data, 20% validation data, and 20% held-out test data for final model evaluation.

Contrastive self-supervised pretraining

Contrastive self-supervised representation learning has demonstrated impressive representation learning capabilities on large amounts of unlabeled data.

Most modern contrastive learning methods are based on objectives that group representations of multiple views (obtained by applying different augmentations) of the same sample closely together while representations of other examples in the dataset are pushed away.

Example of the basic contrastive setup

Swapping Assignments between multiple Views (SwAV)[1], the contrastive framework used in this work, is based on a clustering objective, where representations are assigned to clusters and the network has to learn to predict cluster assignments.

Contrastive model evaluation

We used three common evaluation scenarios from the literature to compare contrastive weights to ImageNet weights:

  • k-nearest neighbors (kNN) evaluation of feature vectors
  • Linear evaluation of feature vectors
  • End-to-end finetuning of the entire ResNet50 network

Results

Micro AUROCs for different evaluation strategies

Micro AUROCs for finetuned models on individual classes

Discussion and future direction

This work presents an initial exploration of self-supervised contrastive learning for satellite data. Our initial results do not support the use of contrastive learning for this application, with improvements over transfer learning from natural image features being marginal or not present at all, while requiring significant computation to train.

However, these results are preliminary, and more extensive evaluation and hyperparameter tuning may close the observed performance gap.

Our results, both with contrastive and ImageNet transfer learning, do show strong performance improvements over previous models for this task. This has immediate consequences for ocean surface layer estimation, where providing a highly reliable model can help with automated analysis and downstream remote sensing applications.

References

[1] Mathilde Caron, Hugo Touvron, Ishan Misra, Hervé Jégou, Julien Mairal, Piotr Bojanowski, and Armand Joulin. Emerging properties in self-supervised vision transformers, 2021.
[2] Chen Wang, Pierre Tandeo, Alexis Mouche, Justin E Stopa, Victor Gressani, Nicolas Longépé, Douglas Vandemark, Ralph C Foster, and Bertrand Chapron. Classification of the global sentinel-1 SAR vignettes for ocean surface process studies. Remote Sensing of Environment, 234:111457, 2019

output:
Identifying the Context Shift between Test Benchmarks and Production Data

Matt Groh, MIT Media Lab

MIT Media Lab

Will your model work in production?

Example errors on out-of-distribution data

Consider the Data Generating Process

Distribution Shift Perspective

Covariate Shift: Ptest(x) ≠ Ptrain(x) and Ptest(y|x) = Ptrain(y|x)

Prior Probability Shift: Ptest(y) ≠ Ptrain(y) and Ptest(x|y) = Ptrain(x|y)

Concept Shift: Ptest(x) = Ptrain(x) and Ptest(y|x) ≠ Ptrain(y|x)

Other Distribution Shift: Ptest(x, y) ≠ Ptrain(x, y)

Context Shift Perspective

Sample Selection Bias: when the data distribution is missing relevant examples or dimensions

Non-stationarity: when the data distribution changes over time or environment

Adversarial Perturbations: when the distribution of data is altered in a way that does not affect human task performance

Contextual Dimensions in Human Centered ML Applications

skin color in face recognition (Buolamwini & Gebru 2018) and dermatology diagnosis (Groh et al 2021, Daneshjou et al, 2022), background scenery for affect recognition (Kosti et al, 2019), number of people in a video for deepfake detection (Groh et al 2022), number of chronic illnesses for algorithmic healthcare risk prediction (Obermeyer et al, 2019), data artifacts like surgical markings (Winkler et al 2019) or clinically irrelevant labels (Oakden-Rayner et al 2020) for medical diagnosis classification, and patients' demographics and self reports of pain for quantifying severity of knee osteoarthritis (Pierson et al, 2021)

Who is represented in the data? Who is annotating the data? When and where is data collected?
How do social, geographical, temporal, aesthetic, financial, and other idiosyncrasies influence the data?

Data Generation Process Desiderata for Dynamic Benchmarks

  • 1. Prediction Task: What are the input features and output labels?
  • 2. Test Size: What is the minimum test size for reducing within data sampling error to an acceptably small rate?
  • 3. Ground Truth Annotation Arbitration: Who has the authority to annotate the data? How should disagreement be represented?
  • 4. Data Inclusion and Exclusion Criteria: What are the possible data sources? How is data curated? What are the quality constraints?

e.g. Dynabench13

Case Study of Implicit Assumptions in Facial Expression Recognition

Example Performance Evaluation of FER from Mollahosseini et al 2016

How Should Facial Expressions be Represented?

As the following "basic emotion categories:" Neutral, Happiness, Sadness, Anger, Fear, Disgust, Surprise? Or as the emotions on the right from Cowen et al 2020? Or something else?

Too many degrees of freedom + too many assumptions

What data can serve as ground truth: posed photographs with observers' labels, frames from a video with observers' labeled peak expressions, self reports, mappings of facial action units to categories? When should we expect the model to generalize? What limits should be express?

VectorMapNet: End-to-end Vectorized HD Map Learning

Yicheng Liu, Tianyuan Yuan, Yue Wang, Yilun Wang, Hang Zhao

Tsinghua University
github.com/tsinghua-mars/VectorMapNet

Motivations

Autonomous driving systems require an understanding of map elements on the road, including lanes, pedestrian crossing, and traffic signs, to navigate around the world.

Involves a complex pipeline
Costly and labor intensive
Needs timely update

In contrast, we focus on using onboard sensors, including LiDARs and cameras, to estimate map elements on-the-fly.

Simple and end-to-end pipeline
Cheap and automatic
Perceives dynamic environment

Contributions

  • VectorMapNet is an end-to-end mapping approach.
  • We utilize polyline to accommodate the heterogeneous nature of map elements.
  • Our method achieves promising performance in online semantic HD map learning tasks.

Problem Formulation

Similar to HDMapNet, our task is to generate vectorized map elements using data from onboard sensors of autonomous vehicle, such as RGB cameras and/or LiDARs. These map elements include:

  • Lane dividers: boundaries dividing lanes on the road, usually straight lines.
  • Road boundaries: boundaries of roads separating roads and sidewalks, typically irregularly-shaped curves of arbitrary lengths.
  • Pedestrian crossings: regions with white markings indicating legal pedestrian crossing points, typically represented as polygons.
  • Centerlines: line segments that commonly used for driving direction, vehicle positioning, and navigation.

Why use polyline to represent maps?

Using polylines to represent map elements has three main advantages:

  • Polylines are a flexible primitive that can represent complex geometric elements in HD maps.
  • The order of polyline vertices is a natural way to encode the direction of map elements.
  • The polyline representation has been widely used by downstream tasks, such as motion forecasting.

VectorMapNet

VectorMapNet, an end-to-end model, is designed to represent a map with a sparse set of polylines thus formulating the task as a sparse set detection problem. In our approach, we divide the task into three distinct components:

(1) A BEV Feature Extractor (A variant of IPM) that lifts various sensor modality inputs into a canonical feature space (BEV space).

(2) A map element detector (DETR-like Transformer decoder) that locates and classifies all map elements by predicting element keypoints Ai and their class labels li.

(3) A Polyline Generator (Vanilla Transformer decoder) that produces a sequence of ordered polyline vertices which describes the local geometry of each detected map element Ai, li.

VectorMapNet for Motion Forecasting

To evaluate the capacity of our method and to investigate its usefulness in downstream tasks, we put our predicted HD map to the test within a motion forecasting task.

Quantitative Results

We report the average precision that uses Chamfer distance and Fréchet Distance as the threshold.

Qualitative Results

Using polylines as primitives has brought us two benefits compared with baselines:

  • Polylines effectively encode the detailed map geometries, e.g., the corners of boundaries (red ellipses).
  • Polyline representations prevent Model from generating ambiguous results, as it consistently encodes direction information. In contrast, Rasterized methods are prone to falsely generating loopy curves (blue ellipses).

Centerline Generation

To demonstrate the flexibility of polyline, we expand VectorMapNet to predict centerlines.

A benefit of posing map learning as a detection problem: The model can identify pedestrian crossings at intersections that are missed in the annotations of the HD map provided by the dataset.

Reference

HDMapNet: An Online HD Map Construction and Evaluation Framework, Li et al., ICRA2022
Structured bird's-eye-view traffic scene understanding from onboard images, Can et al., ICCV 2021

output:
Towards Unbiased Gravitational-Wave Parameter Estimation using SLIC

Ronan Legin, Maximiliano Isi, Kaze Wong, Alexandre Adam, Laurence Perreault-Levasseur, Yashar Hezaveh

Center for Computational Astrophysics, Flatiron Institute, Mila - Quebec Artificial Intelligence Institute, Institute, Ciela - Montreal Institute for Astrophysical Data Analysis and Machine Learning
github.com/ronanlegin/slic

Summary/Objectives

Gravitational wave (GW) parameter estimation demands critical assumptions of noise characteristics in detectors like LIGO, Virgo, and KAGRA. In this study, we utilize score-based diffusion models to empirically learn the noise distribution straight from detector data, and use this to build an unbiased model of the likelihood function.

Introduction

Traditional GW parameter estimation assumes the instrumental noise to be Gaussian and stationary (B. P. Abbott et al., 2020). However, real noise often deviates from a stationary Gaussian due to instrument evolution, data contamination by transient non-Gaussian excursions and nonlinear evolution of narrow spectral features (B. P. Abbott et al., 2016a,b; Buikema et al., 2020). These contaminants can only be managed by specific treatments, resulting in computational expense and potential biases. This work presents a new inference framework for GW parameter estimation, dismissing the need for stationary Gaussian noise assumptions.

Methodology

Given a set of samples {x_i} from the underlying distribution of noise Q, the objective is to infer the score function ∇_x log Q(x). This score function is learned using a neural network through the process of denoising score matching (Hyvärinen, 2005; Vincent, 2011; Song et al., 2020). Once learned, it can be used in a diffusion scheme to generate new samples of noise as shown in Fig. 1.

In the context of score-based likelihood characterization (Legin et al., 2023), the score of the likelihood can be expressed as:

∇_θ log p(x_O | θ) = -∇_x log Q(x) ∇_θ M(θ),

where x_O = M(θ) + x represents an observed GW signal and M(θ) denotes the physical model, accepting the parameters of interest, θ, as input. Throughout inference, x are residuals between the observation and the model, and their statistics should follow the noise if the signal model is accurate.

Figura 1: One-sided noise power spectral density (PSD) of LIGO noise as estimated from 1024 samples of synthetic 4s-long segments of noise generated by our score-based model trained on 11 h of LIGO noise (SLIC, blue), compared to a mean Welch estimate measured empirically from 1 h of real LIGO Hanford noise recorded after the data used in training and not overlapping with it (Welch, dashed gray).

Data

We train the score-based network on 11 h of actual LIGO data around GW150914 (GPS time 1126259462.423 s) obtained from the Gravitational Wave Open Science Center (R. Abbott et al., 2021; Abbot et al., 2023). We split these data into 4 s segments for training, taking care to discard the one segment containing the true signal.

Results

We check that the SLIC likelihood can infer source parameters without bias. To this end, we inject simulated signals with known parameters into real LIGO noise not seen in training, and estimate the parameters using both our model and a standard Gaussian likelihood assuming a Welch PSD as in Fig. 1. For this example, we have chosen a segment of real noise data without significant glitches, such that the Gaussian likelihood also recovers the true parameters. As shown, the SLIC posterior is capable of recovering the true injected values with high credibility.

Figura 2: Posterior distribution of the five recovered parameters of a simulated signal using SLIC (orange) and the standard Gaussian likelihood (blue). The contours are the 10%, 39.35% and 90% interval. The true values are marked by the black lines. The posterior distribution using SLIC has a smaller variance compared to the Gaussian likelihood.

Discussion and conclusions

We are working on a number of improvements before applying our framework to real signals contaminated by instrumental glitches, such as GW170817.

  • We plan to enhance the current model to generate larger segments (from 4 to 128 seconds) to analyze binary neutron stars.
  • Instead of analyzing data from a single detector, we intend to coherently model data from a network of detectors to improve parameter estimation.
  • We aim to improve the generalization of the model by training on larger quantities of data spanning months of detector data.
  • We aim to boost inference efficiency by transitioning from MALA to FLOWMC (Wong et al., 2023).

References

Abbott, R., et al. (2023). Gravitational wave open science center (1126216262-1126302662). Retrieved from https://gwosc.org/archive/links/01/
Abbott, B. P., et al. (2016a). Characterization of transient noise in Advanced LIGO relevant to gravitational wave signal GW150914. Class. Quant. Grav., 33(13), 134001.
Abbott, B. P., et al. (2016b). Sensitivity of the Advanced LIGO detectors at the beginning of gravitational wave astronomy. Phys. Rev. D, 93(11), 112004.
Abbott, B. P., et al. (2020). A guide to LIGO-Virgo detector noise and extraction of transient gravitational-wave signals. Class. Quant. Grav., 37(5), 055002.
Buikema, A., et al. (2020). Sensitivity and performance of the Advanced LIGO detectors in the third observing run. Phys. Rev. D, 102(6), 062003.
Hyvärinen, A. (2005, dec). Estimation of non-normalized statistical models by score matching. J. Mach. Learn. Res., 6, 695–709.
Legin, R., Adam, A., Hezaveh, Y., & Perreault Levasseur, L. (2023, February). Beyond Gaussian Noise: A Generalized Approach to Likelihood Analysis with non-Gaussian Noise. arXiv e-prints, arXiv:2302.03046.
Song, Y., Sohl-Dickstein, J., Kingma, D. P., Kumar, A., Ermon, S., & Poole, B. (2020, November). Score-Based Generative Modeling through Stochastic Differential Equations. arXiv e-prints, arXiv:2011.13456.
Wong, K. W. K., Gabrié, M., & Foreman-Mackey, D. (2023). flowMC: Normalizing flow enhanced sampling package for probabilistic inference in JAX. J. Open Source Softw., 8(83), 5021.
ronan.legin@umontreal.ca
misi@flatironinstitute.org
kwong@flatironinstitute.org

output:
Scaling Spherical CNNs

Carlos Esteves, Jean-Jacques Slotine, Ameesh Makadia

Google Research
MIT
machc@google.com
jjs@mit.edu
makadia@google.com
github.com/google-research/spherical-cnn

Introduction

Spherical CNNs
  • Principled way of learning from spherical signals, using spherical convolutions computed spectrally.
  • Equivariant to 3D rotations.
  • Respects spherical topology, robust to sampling.

Cosmic radiation

Cyclone tracks

Sea surface temperature

Spherical panorama (SUN360)

Contributions
  • Spherical CNNs 100x larger than previously possible.
  • More expressive and efficient layers and activations.
  • Application-specific modeling for molecules and weather.
Related work
Cohen et al (ICLR'18), Esteves et al (ECCV'18, NeurIPS'20), Kondor et al (NeurIPS'18), Cobb et al (ICLR'21), McEwen et al (ICLR'22), Ocampo et al (ICLR'23).

Method

  • Based on spin-weighted SCNNs (Esteves et al, NeurIPS'20).
  • Phase collapse activation: x₀ ← W₁x₀ + W₂|x| + b
  • Efficient spin-weighted spherical harmonics on TPUs.
  • Spectral batch normalization, pooling and residual connection.

Spin-weighted spherical convolution as a vector field

Molecular property regression

  • Represent a molecule as a set of spheres per atom, regress a set of properties.
  • One feature channel per atom type.
  • Use directional attributes only (no radius).

f_{iz}(x) = \sum_{z_j=z} \frac{z_i z_j}{|r_{ij}|^p} e^{-\frac{(x-r_{ij})^2}{\beta |r_{ij}|}}

Weather forecasting

  • Take a set of atmospheric variables like temperature, wind, humidity, and predict their future values.
  • WeatherBench (Rasp et al): predict directly 3 or 5 days ahead.
  • Keisler (arXiv'22): predict iteratively in 6h increments.

Similar patterns may appear at different positions and orientations; equivariance allows filter reuse.

AUTOMATIC CLIPPING: DIFFERENTIALLY PRIVATE DEEP LEARNING MADE EASIER AND STRONGER

Zhiqi Bu, Yu-Xiang Wang, Sheng Zha, George Karypis

Amazon
github.com/amazon-science/dp-optimizer

ABSTRACT

Per-example gradient clipping is a key algorithmic step that enables practical differential privacy (DP) training for deep learning models. The choice of clipping threshold R is shown to be vital for achieving high accuracy under DP but difficult to tune.

We propose an easy-to-use replacement, called Automatic Clipping, that eliminates the need to tune R for any DP optimizers.

DIFFERENTIAL PRIVACY IN DEEP LEARNING

Differential privacy (DP) is a formal definition of privacy that has been shown to prevent the privacy risks in deep learning.

Definition 1. A randomized algorithm M is (ε, δ)-DP if for any neighboring datasets S, S'...

DP Optimizer({gi}) = Optimizer(Σ gi · Clip(||gi||; R) + σR · N(0, I))

PER-SAMPLE GRADIENT CLIPPING

Abadi's clipping is the most widely applied clipping function, which requires a hyperparameter - the clipping threshold R.

CONTRIBUTIONS

  • We propose a new per-sample gradient clipping function that does not require the clipping threshold R.
  • We give a convergence analysis of automatic DP-SGD in the non-convex setting.
  • We demonstrate on extensive language and vision tasks that automatic clipping outperforms or matches state-of-the-art.
  • We demonstrate minimal changes to adopt our automatic clipping in existing codebases.

R-INDEPENDENCE OF AUTOMATIC CLIPPING

We show that using automatic clipping, it suffices to tune learning rate and weight decay in DP training as in the regular training.

Theorem 1. Non-adaptive R-dependent automatic DP-SGD is equivalent to R-independent automatic DP optimizers.

Theorem 2. Adaptive R-dependent automatic DP optimizers (including AdaGrad, Adam, etc.) are equivalent to R-independent automatic DP optimizers.

CONVERGENCE ANALYSIS OF AUTOMATIC DP-SGD

We analyze the convergence on non-convex loss with standard assumptions in SGD literature.

Theorem 3. Under Assumption 3-5, running automatic DP-SGD for T iterations...

Leveraging Theorem 4, our analysis provides guidelines to DP training that match empirical observation.

AUTOMATIC CLIPPING IS EASY TO CODE

Switching from Abadi's clipping to automatic clipping changes only one line of code.

per_sample_clip_factor = self.max_grad_norm / (per_sample_norms + 0.01)

EMPIRICAL RESULTS

References: [1] Abadi et al. Deep learning with differential privacy. CCS 2016.

Meinshausen and P. Bühlmann (2006). \"High-dimensional graphs and variable selection with the Lasso\". In: Institute of Mathematical Statistics", "H. Wang (2012). \"Bayesian Graphical Lasso Models and Efficient Posterior Computation.\" In: Bayesian Anal. 7 (4)", "G. Papamakarios et al (2021). \"Normalizing Flows for Probabilistic Modeling and Inference.\" In: Journal of Machine Learning Research 22"], "tables": [], "figures": [{"fig_id": "fig_7", "description": "QR code for project repository", "figure_width": 150, "figure_height": 150, "figure_caption": []}]}]}
output:
Conditional Matrix Flows for Gaussian Graphical Models

Marcello Massimo Negri, Fabricio Arend Torres, Volker Roth

Department of Computer Science, University of Basel, Switzerland
marcellomassimo.negri@unibas.ch
github.com/unibas-dmi-hpc/cmf

Gaussian Graphical Models: Graphical Lasso

  • GGMs provide structural information: Ωij = 0 implies conditional independence of features i and j given all remaining features
  • Maximum likelihood estimate of Ω is not sparse and for d > n is not unique → penalized likelihood to encourage sparse Ω

argmaxΩ>0 {log det Ω - Tr(1/n SΩ) - λ||Ω||1}

Intractable because the objective is highly non-convex

Graphical Lasso: use convex relaxation ||Ω||0 → ||Ω||1

Benefits

  • Solution path: frequentist approach provides solutions as a function of λ

Limitations

  • Over-shrinking: l1 norm causes well-known over-shrinking of features
  • No credibility interval

Bayesian Graphical Lasso

  • Bayesian approach: p(Ω|S, λ) ∝ p(S|Ω) p(Ω|λ)
  • Encourage sparsity with double exponential prior (DE)

p(S|Ω) ∝ Wd(n, Ω⁻¹)
p(Ω|λ) ∝ Wd(d + 1, λ1d) ∏ DE(ωij|λ) I[Ω > 0]

Benefits

  • Credibility interval: can be computed within Bayesian framework

Limitations

  • Expensive posterior sampling: needs Gibbs sampler
  • Over-shrinking: prior cannot be generalized to sub-l1 pseudo-norms
  • Expensive solution path: independent Markov chains for each λ value

Conditional Matrix Flow for Gaussian Graphical Models

Goal: unify benefits of frequentist and Bayesian frameworks while overcoming limitations

We propose a general framework for variational inference with matrix-variate Normalizing Flows in GGMs. As a key improvement on previous work, we train with one flow a continuum of sparse regression models jointly for all regularization parameters λ and all lq norms, including non-convex sub-l1 pseudo-norms.

VARIATIONAL INFERENCE
  • Posterior approximation and efficient sampling via Normalizing Flow
SUB-lq PSEUDO-NORMS
  • Generalized Gaussian Distributions (GGD) as sparsity-inducing priors
CONDITIONAL FLOW
  • Condition the Normalizing Flow on λ and q to obtain solution paths

ARCHITECTURE

Combine benefits

  • Credibility intervals: sample-based
  • Solution paths: recover Bayesian GL

Overcome limitations

  • Cheap posterior sampling: no Gibbs
  • No over-shrinking: GGD prior

References

N. Meinshausen and P. Bühlmann (2006). "High-dimensional graphs and variable selection with the Lasso". In: Institute of Mathematical Statistics

H. Wang (2012). "Bayesian Graphical Lasso Models and Efficient Posterior Computation." In: Bayesian Anal. 7 (4)

G. Papamakarios et al (2021). "Normalizing Flows for Probabilistic Modeling and Inference." In: Journal of Machine Learning Research 22

output:
Differentially Private CutMix for Split Learning with Vision Transformer

Seungeun Oh, Jihong Park, Sihun Baek, Hyelin Nam, Praneeth Vepakomma, Ramesh Raskar, Medhi Bennis, and Seong-Lyun Kim

Yonsei University
Deakin University
MIT
Neural Information Processing Systems
github.com/yonsei-ml/DP-CutMixSL

Motivation

(a) Input raw image.

(b) Smashed data of ViT.

(c) Smashed data of CNN.

  • Recently, vision transformer (ViT) outpace CNN in CV field [1]. Split learning (SL) [2] with model partitioning is suitable for its large memory size and computing cost.
  • Objective: to design privacy-preserving distributed ML considering differences between ViT and CNN.

1. ViT often omits pooling: Efficient regularization on smashed data, but privacy leak.

2. ViT learns global spatial information: Robust to large noise partially applied to the image (i.e. CutMix [3]).

3. ViT runs at patch-level: Possibilities of a patch-scale regularizer.

→ Integrating the above yields a patch-level randomized CutMix of hidden representations (patch CutMix).

  • To this end, we propose DP-CutMixSL, which combines patch CutMix with the differentially private (DP) SL framework.
[1] A. Dosovitskiy, et al. An image is worth 16x16 words: Transformers for image recognition at scale. arXiv:2010.11929
[2] P. Vepakomma, et al. Split learning for health: Distributed deep learning without sharing raw patient data. arXiv:1812.00564
[3] S. Yun, et al. Cutmix: Regularization strategy to train strong classifiers with localizable features. IEEE/CVF ICCV 2019
[4] I. Mironov. Rényi differential privacy. IEEE CSF 2017

Proposed: DP-CutMixSL

Algorithm 1 DP-CutMixSL

requirements: w = [w_c,i, w_s] (w_c,i: lower model segment, w_s: upper model segment)
while w not converged do
  /* Runs on mixer */
  samples {a1, ..., an} ~ Dir(alpha)
  generates pseudo random sequences Mi for all i
  /* Runs on client i */
  generates smashed data si by passing input xi through w_c,i
  produces s'_i by masking si via Mi
  uploads s'_i to the server
  /* Runs on server */
  produces s'_v via aggregation for all i
  updates w_s via backprop
end while

DP-CutMixSL's Operation:

  • 1. Gaussian noise mechanism: Each device first injects random Gaussian noise into smashed data.
  • 2. Random Cutout: Each device punches randomly selected patches of smashed data, yielding Cutout smashed data.
  • 3. Random Mixup: These Cutout smashed data are uploaded to and put together by the server, resulting in DP-CutMix smashed data that continue FP.

Differential Privacy Analysis

Theorem 1. The RDP bounds [4] are given as:

ε_o(α) = α/2 * (Δ²Dx/σx² + Dy/σy²)
ε_Mix(α) = ... (complex RDP formula)
ε_CutMix(α) = ... (combined RDP formula)

  • 1. Large privacy leakage in ViT: Without pooling, smashed data size Dx is large.
  • 2. Cutout & Mixing amplify DP: Mixup is the best for privacy, but accuracy. CutMix is a good compromise.
  • 3. Hide in the crowd: Many clients inherently preserve privacy.

Numerical Evalution

  • 1. CutMix SL achieves highest accuracy (Top)
  • 2. Privacy-Accuracy trade-off, in terms of:
    • - Mixup vs. Cutout (Left)
    • - Mixing Group Size vs. Accuracy (Right)
